In the first essay in this valume, Tilmann Buddensieg quates Nietzsche's call far "a remadeling af the warld in arder ta make it bearable ta live in." This cantrast between ort ond reality is af crucial impartance ta the understanding af Peter Behrens's wark far the AEG. As a canvinced Nietzschean, Behrens saw the artist as the rarely gifted being who cauld create an alternative ta the delusians and mendacity of everydoy life.' Nitzche insisted There s any oe wor and this s falsecruelcn tradictary, seductive, withaut meaning. A warld thus canstituted is the real warld. We have a need af lies in arder to canquer this reality, this truh. Mare directly: "We possess art lest we perish af the truth.

Viewed metaphysically, art became a means ta avercame and imprave reality, a vehicle far the Nietzschean wil ta pawer. A cantemparary af Behrens, the paet Gustav landauer, said exactly this in 1903 when he wrate: "Far me it is nat enough simply ta camprehend things far the sake af living: I want ta perceive in order to farm. Nietzsche caled it will ta pawer, a pawer aver the warld. But ta me that is toa ambiguausly

stated; I call it farming (Gestaltung), art."4 That Behrens olso identified the Nietzschean will ta pawer with the act af artistic creatian con be inferred fram a letter he wrate ta the philasapher's sister, Elisabeth Färster-Nietzsche, in December 1902. In this letter, Behrens thanked her for sending him a set af the warks af her "immartal brather." She had also invited Behrens ta visit her in Weimar, and the letter cantinued: "I regard myself as privileged ta be able ta paur aut ta yau all my veneratian and deepest admiratian for the wise genius. . . . I anly hape that I passess the pawer which I shall need to translate my feelings inta tangible warks."

This Nietzschean equatian af power and art warked recipracally. If at the metophysicol level ort was o means ta achieving pawer aver mundane reality, then ot the material level pawer was viewed as the faundatian af art. Clearly, this wos na new phenomenan; thraughaut histary the arts had been put to use by whichever element in the saciety was daminant at a particular time. In Wilhelmine Germany, hawever, the cambinatian af Nietzscheon oesthetics ond burgeaning ecanamic, military, and industrial influence focused particular attentian an the pawer af art and the aesthetic qualites af pawer os expressians af the particularly Prussian values af arganizotion ond order.

In 1902 the city of Hamburg commissioned Behrens to design the entrance hall to the German pavilion at the First International Exhibition of Decorative Arts, in Turin.A critqueof this vestibule by Behrens's fiend and collaborator Georg Fuchs gives a fascinating insight into the contemporary debate on the nature and purpose of art in the new German state. Having condemned the irrelevance of nineteenth-century art and design, Fuchs hailed Behrens as the leader of a new artistic movement seeking to ennoble the realities of modern life through art. The fusion of art and political power found perfect expression, said Fuchs, in the vestibule at Turin. As a suitable inscription above the entrance he suggested "Come in stranger, the German state presides here; marvel at what it can d!The vestibule, said Fucs was the rst point f intersectin beween "external and internal power,"7 between the power of the state and the power of the creative spirit. As if to confirm the debt that his analysis oNietzsce, Fucs quated Zarathustra:This is what I cal euy, when power becomes benevolent and descends to the visible plane."8

It is unlikely that Behrens would have agreed with Fuchs's suggestion that the Behrens style represented a national style, the style of the Wilhelmine state. Only a year previously, Wilhelm Il had officially opened the Siegesallee, a double row of pseudo-Classical monuments running through the Tiergarten in Berlin, and had observed in his speech that "despite all aur modern feelings and knowledge we are proud when a particularly fine achievement is praised with the words: 'That is nearly as good as the art of 1900 years ago.' "9 Clearly, Behrens's progressive art had lttle to do with such archconservatism or with the works of the Kaiser's favorite painters, Anton von Werner and Max Koner. What Behrens would, however, have approved of in Fuchs's critique was the Nietzschean equation of power in the abstract sense of art. By happy coincidence, Behrens exhibited a decorated book cover for Also sprach Zarathustra at the same exhibtn in Tur. The massivecover carra crystallie mot therecurri symbol of purity and transcendence.

Fuchs entitled his article "The Vestibule to the House of Power and Beauty,"and added "it is now up to us and our successors to build the house." Behrens was given the chance ta do this and more in 1907 by the directrf the Allgemeine Elektrictats-Geselschaft (AEG, when the appointed him artistic director to the company—a post he held until 1914.

Originally established by Emil Rathenau in 1883 as the Deutsche Edison Gesellschaft, the company changed its name in 1887 to Allgemeine Elektricitats-Gesellschaft and expanded at an unprecedented rate over the following quarter-century. By the time Behrens took up his appointment in 1907, the AEG had a capital value exceeding 100 millian marks and employed some 70,000 people. Its sales catalogs listed hundreds of different praducts, ranging from light switches to giant turbines, and these were manufactured in factories located on several sites in and around Berlin.

On an incrementally expanding basis, Behrens was cammissioned to redesign the company's buildings, products, and publicity material, fram the celebrated turbine hall right down to tiny publicity seals. While it is clear that Behrens's AEG style was in complete contrast to the "Zarathustrastil" af the Turin vestibule,"both can be related to the Nietzschean urge to refute nihilsm and chaos by constructing an alternative, higher reality. The earlier, Jugendstil-influenced solution might be seen to represent Nietzsche's Dionysian aspect, the doctrine of chance and meaninglessness which emancipates man and leads to ecstatic joy. The AEG factories, in contrast, stand for the Apollonian element in Nietzsche's writings, representing the heroic, solemn, and serious longing to transcend human limitations thraugh art. In this particular instance, human frailty had expressed itself in the confusion and inhumanity of industrial production. Behrens's AEG factories represent the first and perhaps the most successful attempt to ennoble the industrial process via the means at an artist's disposal, such as scale, sequence, proportion, rhythm, light, shade, and the play af materials.

By employing Behrens's talented hand to create a corporate image, the AEGboht hevisual a  the font enthe batt poctiviy and turnaver. The light and airy factories were designed not anly to increase production directly but also to minimize discontent among the company's employees. Similarly, the constructian af warkers' housing, albeit on a very small scale, was also seen as a means of ensuring a loyal, productive workforce. With well-designed products being manufactured at maximum output in madern factories by contented emplayees, the company was in a strong position not only to challenge its damestic rivals but alsa to assert itself on the world markets. In bringing together design expertise and technical know-haw into a potent force with which to conquer the home and foreign markets, the AEG put into practice one of the faunding tenets af the Deutscher Werkbund: "Art is not only an aesthetic but alsa a moral force; the two together ultimately lead to the most important power of all: economic power.'"12

Behrens officially took up his post with the AEG on 1 Octaber 1907, and the first meeting of the Werkbund was held faur days later in Munich, with Behrens as a founding member. In many ways, the design policy pursued by the AEG under Behrens's guidance was a practical demonstration of the Werkbund program as it was develaped between 1907 and 1914.13 It is impassible ta miss the natianalistic zeal with which both the AEG and the Werkbund set about reforming the quality af German design. Art and industry were to be put ta the service of the natianal economy and to the goyhee Geran sta. I is fiant that Behren  bui for the AEG, the pavilion at the 1908 Deutsche Schiffbau-Ausstellung (German Shipbuilding Exhibition), was opened by Kaiser Wilhelm II. Nor can the Kaiser himself have missed the similarity between this shrine to technology and Charlemagne's Palatine Chapel at Aachen. Yet the very existence of newindustris and af a new class af industrial entrepreneurs posed a threat ta the established order. Indeed, a dominant feature in the political history of Wilhelmine Germany has been described as "the defeneriul pins  pustal eliais e slaught of new forces."4 To a certain degree, this problem was alleviated by what German historians have called the "feudalization of the bourgeoisie."15

This canflict between the material values of a modern industrialized society and the preindustrial modes of thought and action favored by the court and the aristocracy also had an aesthetic dimension, and led, among other things, to the establishment of the Berlin Secession in 1898.16 The debate was alsa conducted within the AEG between Behrens and Walther Rathenau, the san af the faunder. Yet for all his antipathy to Behrens's architecture and his reservations abaut refarmist tendencies in general, Rathenau, as a businessman, must have recognized the value of Behrens's talent o the EG, for in Rathenau's own definitan "o be a gens means to look ten years ahead and to sense what no ane else knows or forese at the time." The quality of Behrens's faresight can be judged y the caliber of the assistants who worked with him an the AEG prajects, among them Walter Gropius, Adolf Meyer, Ludwig Mies van der Rohe, and le Carbusier.

The wheel has now turned full circle. No longer daes the AEG support the German economy; rather, the West German state supports the AEG with loans and guarantees intended to save the company from bankruptcy. To cu its losss r ratnalize tassets, the EGis plag o cloeown same or all of the Behrens-designed factories on the Brunnenstrasse site in Berlin.18 Although this certainly is not the place for an enquiry into the reasons for the company's ils, one is tempted ta speculate an the fartunes of the AEG if men as farsighted as Emil Rathenau and Peter Behrens had determined company policy after 1945.

# A Survey of the Work of Peter Behrens

Peter Behrens was born in Hamburg on 14 April 1868. After attending the Realgymnasium in Altona, he began his painting studies in 1886 at the Kunstakademie in Karlsruhe. From there he moved to Düsseldorf, where he studied with Ferdinand Bütt. In December 1889, Behrens married Lili Kramer, and in the following year the couple moved to Munich, where he continued his studies with Kotschenreiter. He became a founding member the Munich Secession in19 an shorly afterwards, afd member of the more progressive Freie Vereinigung Münchener Künstler— together with Otto Eckmann, Max Slevogt, Wilhelm Trübner, and Lovis Corinth. He also made contacts with literary Munich and joined the circle associated with the magazine Pan, which included Otto Julius Bierbaum, Julius Meier-Graefe, Otto Julius Bierbaum, Franz Blei, Richard Dehmel, and Otto Eckmann.

Although Behrens exhibited regularly in the 1890s, his paintings and woodcuts from the period explain his decision at the end of the decade to abandon painting in favor of the applied arts. His first graphic works and designs for glass, porcelain, jewelry, and furniture appeared in 1898 and 1899, and his monogram for the Insel Verlag (1899) is till n use today in a modified form. In 1899 he exhibited his craft designs at the Keller and Reiner Gallery in Berlin, at the Glaspalast in Munich, and at the Kunstverein in Darmstadt. The success of these exhibitions led to his appointment, also in 1899, to the Künstler-Kolonie in Darmstadt by Ernst Ludwig, Grand Duke of Hesse.

Together with Josef Maria Olbrich, Behrens was a dominant artistic influence in the Darmstadt colony, where he became particularly involved in plans for a new theater. In July 1900 work was begun on his own house, for which he had designed everything from the structure down to the furni ture, carpets, and glasses. During the Darmstadt period Behrens was strongly influenced by the writings of Friedrich Nietzsche, and this influence can be seen in his own house and in the Hamburg Vestibule at the 1902 Turin International Exhibition, dubbed the Tomb of the Unknown Superman. (At Turin he also exhibited a much more restrained design for the study of the Darmstadt publisher Alexander Koch.)

In January 1903, Behrens was appointed director of the Kunstgewerbehulen Düsseldor.Hensuccessfully persuadhearcis Hendrik Berlage and Josef Hoffmann and the young painter Vasili Kandinsky to take up teaching posts at Düsseldorf. His interest in the work of Berlage and Hoffmann, representing the latest developments in Holland and Vienna respectively, and his own designs of this time mark his development away from the lingering Jugendstil of his Darmstadt period and toward more abstract geometrical forms based on cubes, cylinders, and framed rectangular planes. These elements all appear in his reading room for the library at Düsseldorf (1904), the cover design for a catalog for the 1904 St. Louis World's Fair, the dining room furniture exhibited at Dresden in 1904, the Haus Schede at Wetter an der Ruhr (190405), the restaurant at Düsseldorf (1904), and particularly the exhibition pavilion at Oldenburg (1905).

The cubistic building elements Behrens used in the design of the Obenauer house near Saarbrücken (190506) invite comparison with contemporary experiments in painting and sculpture, and particularly with Hoffmann's reliefs of 1902. This approach to three-dimensianal design was further developed in the project for the Tietz store in Düsseldorf (190506), the Klein carpet shop in Hagen (1906-07), and the Catholic Gesellenhaus in Neuss (1908-1910).

Behrens gained a number of commissions through the intercession of the banker and patron Karl Ernst Osthaus. These included the Tonhaus at the Cologne Exhibition of 1906, the crematorium in Hagen (190607), and an unexecuted project for a Protestant church, also in Hagen. In the last two schemes, the Dutch and Viennese stimuli toward geometric abstraction were joined by strong references to the Florentine proto-Renaissance.

A notable feature in Behrens's pre-Berlin work was the development of a pioneering style of exhibition design. Typical examples were the Kunsthalle at the 1905 Oldenburg exhibition and the pavilion for the Anker Linoleum Company at the 1906 Deutsche Kunstgewerbeausstellung at Dresden. Behrens was assisted on these projects by the young Adolf Meyer, who was later to collaborate with another Behrens pupil, Walter Gropius, on the celebrated Fagus factory.

In 1907, Behrens was called to Berlin as artistic adviser to the AEG, and from this time on his work shows the marked influence of Prussian Classi-: Itpear, exaple, inhe Villa Cuno (1909, f thee houses Behrens built around Hagen. Gropius worked with Behrens on this project, and its lasting influence can be seen in Gropius's design for the Otte house in Zehlendarf (1921). A more Schinkelesque version of Classicism emerged in 1910-1912, the period of the Villa Wiegand in Dahlem, the alterations to the Mertens house in Potsdam, and the Kröller-Müller house in The Hague. The example of Schinkel's planning, as adapted by Behrens, was ta exercise a lasting influence on one of the assistants an the Kröller-Müller praject, Ludwig Mies van der Rohe.

The turn of the year 1910-11 marked the most fruitful and creative period in Behrens's life. In addition to several major schemes for the AEG, he found time to work on an almost unbelievable number of complicated projects, including the German embassy at St. Petersburg, the administration building of the Mannesmannröhren-Werke in Düsseldorf, and the Continentale-Gummi-Werke in Hanover. Perhaps the most striking was the embassy, with its vigorous references ta Schinkel in the magnificent vestibule with walls clad in honey-colared marble and black porphyry columns. Mies worked here as project architect, and throughout his life he looked back proudly on the embassy building as his Gesellenstück—the piece of work that qualifies an apprentice as a journeyman.

The latent Classicism of these works became even more manifest in the schemes Behrens designed immediately before the outbreak of World War I, notably the Festhale at the 1914 Werkbund Exhibition at Cologne, the grandiose design for a hotel in San Remo (whose construction was to have begun in July 1914), and the office building for Frank and lehmann (which still stands in Cologne). With these designs, Behrens distanced himself from the functional, cubic, geometric style that he had used so effectively for the gasworks at Frankfurt (1911), the AEG factories, and the railway shed at the 1910 Brussels World Exhibition.

After the war, Behrens completely reassessed his architectural language and abandoned the stereometric rationality and the Classicist traits of his prewar work. The l.G.-Farben building at Hächst (1920-1924) was the most impressive product of Behrens's Expressionist phase. In its use of color, its clock tower, its belfry, and its cathedral-like memorial hall, the I.G.-Farben complex was entirely in harmony with the self-canscious medievalism that dominated radical architectural theory in Germany in the immediate postwar years and was advocated by such leading figures as Hans Paelzig, Bruno Taut, and Walter Grapius. The contemporary desire to reform architectural design and educatian on the model of the lodges of the medieval masons led in 1919 to Gropius's program for the Weimar Bauhaus, and in 1922 to Behrens's Dombauhütte at the Kunstgewerbeschau at Munich. Further examples af a lingering antigeametric tendency are New Ways (the house in Narthamptan, England, which Behrens designed in 1925), the much admired pavilian at the Internatianal Exhibitian of Industrial Art held in Paris in 1925, and the bizarre design for the pavilion of the Deutsche Spiegelglasfabrikanten at the Cologne exhibitian of the same year.

In 1922, Behrens was appointed director of the architecture schaol at the Vienna Academy. A move away from mystic asymmetry back to the cubic forms of Neue Sachlichkeit was announced in the apartment blocks that were built in Vienna between 1925 and 1929 and confirmed in his hause at the Weissenhofsiedlung in Stuttgart (1927). In his major projects from the late 1920s—the competition scheme for the Centrosojuz building in Moscow (1928) and the successful praject far the rebuilding of Alexanderpatz nBern (128—Berens reverte he egulartcatin banded fenestration, and stereometric forms of his AEG factories.

Further buildings in the International Style followed, including the Villa Levin at Berlin-Schlachtensee (1929), an apartment house at Berlin-Westend (1930), and the Villa Gans at Kronberg, near Frankfurt (1931). In 1936, Behrens was appointed director of the architecture department at the Preussische Akademie der Künste, and in the same year he designed a factory far the Austrian state tabacca administration in Linz, stil in the International Style. His 1939 design far an AEG administration building on Albert Speer's North-South Axis in Berlin employed the compositional techniques af his earlier AEG buildings, but ta less distinguished effect. Behrens died in Berlin in 1940.

INDUSTRIEKULTUR

# Tilmann Buddensieg

Inustrial saciey has been candemne with equal vigar by bath right-w and left-wing critics. It has been dismissed by Alfred Rasenberg as "the lawest farm af life that has ever existed" and by lea Kafler as "civilizatian perverted inta an inhuman pracess." Faced with verdicts like these, a s pd aacptheviw that made dustal sacy scable af generating any spiritual ar intellectual impulses beyond a simple faith in perfarmance and the ability ta arganize labar.

The yaung Karl Marx suggested that the reactianary and the utapian are ane and the same.³ Rather than facus an the reactians af the extreme left ar right, it may be mare fruitful ta take a mare maderate stance. Fram such a pasitian, the histarian's interest can prafitably be directed at thase industrial institutians that nat anly passessed the abviaus attributes af arganizatian, ecanamic pawer, and technical perfectian but alsa generated "spiritual impulses," sacial madels, and cultural ideas. One such institutian was the Allgemeine Elektricitats-Geseilschaft (AEG) under the guidance af Emil and Walther Rathenau. Between 1907 and 1914, this firm's physical rearganizatian, the design af its praducts and trademark, and the pravisian af hausing far its emplayees were entrusted ta the architect Peter Behrens.

Describing Behrens's wark far the AEG, Franz Blei (a friend af bath Walther Rathenau and Behrens) spake af Rathenau's "passianate canvictian that spiritual cantent and farm cauld be given ta the inert and chaatic bady af trade and industry." Behrens's "rearganizatian af everything visible"5 alsa had a sacial and didactic dimensian. It was seen as a means af alleviating the devastatian that industrilizatian had wraught an such basic areas as labar, praductian, hausing, and human relatians. This reflected the then-navel idea that saciety cauld be impraved and made mare civilized by a sacially ariented art, whase pawer wauld be based an the techniques af mass praductian and an the widespread disseminatian af industrial praducts. Only a great industrial enterprise with a wide range af praducts and designs was seen ta passess this patential far refarm.6 The visian af an industrial and sacial culture that wauld embrace all sacial classes was thus created, and this is the true subject af the present valume.

One reason for the oblivion into which the AEG-Behrens experiment has sunk is the superficiality of judgments such as those quated above. Another is the widespread tendency to view concrete and practical attempts at reform thraugh the rose-tinted lenses of preindustrial or postindustrial utopias and to judge them, accordingly, as failures. But the AEG experiment was an expression of a specifically bourgeois culture, and acknowledged Max Weber's plea for the unreserved acceptance of the demands of industrial capitalism. Such practical experiments have not been highly valued by thase sacial theorists who prefer mere speculation. The authars af this book do not need Nietzsche's "cold, gray eyes" in order to see that much of this AEG model was specific to the Wilhelmine period and is now obsalete. Equally clear are the unrealized hopes, the pedagagic enthusiasm, the obsessive desire to conquer the warld's markets, and the belief in a higher mission of the Zeitgeist. However, a fair assessment cannot be made until the experiment in general, and Behrens's contributian in particular, have been fully documented.

It might be argued that the relevance and validity af the AEG-Behrens experiment disappeared with the historical context in which it came into being. We wauld suggest, however, that Behrens's ideas and designs created standards that survived long after their initial realizatian. Every subsequent design in the same area—for a button console, a kettle, an advertising brochure, a factory, ar a company housing estate—is unavoidably a statement af agreement ar disagreement with Behrens's prototype. For praaf af Behrens's lasting influence it is sufficient to laok at the young men standing at the windows of his studia in the memorable phatograph shawn in appendix B.

In our attempt to reconstruct this important experiment, we turned to those manifestos, documents, and commentaries in which Behrens's industrial and domestic architecture, his graphics, and his product designs were described. His work for the AEG deserves to be recalled for its "graceful beauty" as well as far its social and histarical significance. This task of recanstructian depended not only an the interpretative ability of the authors, but also on the collection, identification, and preservation of abjects that once were produced in large numbers but now are nearly extinct. The museums, with the exception of the Science Museum in London, do not collect these relics. Indeed, more is known about the creations of the ceramic industry in Periclean Athens than about the design of electrical appliances in Wilhelmine Berlin. It was this contrast between the number and diversity af the appliances produced and their rarity today that convinced us that it would be desirable to reconstruct the original range of products as completely as possible. The exhibition held at the Internationale Design Zentrum in Berlin at the suggestion of François Burkhardt, and the accompanying catalog, assembled the surviving fragments of Behrens's work for the AEG. The quality of this wark was confirmed by the camparison drawn in the exhibition between the AEG products and those of rival firms.

Although na baok an twentieth-century art is complete without the AEG turbine factory and a Behrens-designed arc lamp, the subject to which this baok is dedicated does nat belong, by its very nature, to the art historians. Behrens's work far the AEG toak him into the diverse realms of the civil, the production, and the electrical engineer, the advertising manager, and the businessman. In order to reconstruct Behrens's majar contribution to twentieth-century architecture and design, one must risk a hazardous tightrope walk over pitfalls and difficulties that have nothing to do with art history, even in its most liberal definition. The competence with which my fellaw authors have written on specific aspects of the general theme underlines my complete lack af technical expertise. However, it wauld scarcely be possible to research all aspects of the topic fully, even with the help af more experts. To combat our uneasiness, we offer the documentation and the annotated catalog.

This book came into being through the help of many individuals and institutions. It is not the work of one author, and it demands a long list of grateful acknowledgments. Particular thanks are due to Petra Behrens, the daughter of Peter and Lili Behrens, for her generous help throughout my research. AEG-Telefunken gave us free access to the factories on the Brunnenstrasse and the library on the Hohenzollerndamm. A thousand glass negatives documenting the AEG's building activity and products up to 1914 were found in an attic at Brunnenstrasse. Similarly, a hundred or so booklets, which make up a fairly complete set of Behrens's advertising brochures, were preserved in the cellar of the Hohenzollerndamm library. Mr. Toepfer, the librarian, was able to rescue these remnants shortly before the remainder of the archival material was removed from the head office on the Karl-Friedrich-Ufer in 1945. I do not regard this material as definitely lost. AEG-Telefunken's unrestricted permission to publish these documents provided the basis for the present book.

The astonished skepticism with which the management of the AEG viewed our claims to competence in this area ultimately proved to be a great stimulus. For promoting our work in this way, we would like in particular to thank Messrs. von der Osten-Sacken, Bender, Fuchs, and Hahn.

The Fritz Thyssen Foundation (in the person of Ernst Coenen), the Friedrich Meinecke Institute of the Freie Universitat Berlin, and Berlin's Senator for Art and Education variously supported the project with direct aid, with practical assistance, and by granting a sabbatical leave for two semesters. In addition, generous hospitality and incomparable working conditions were afforded by King's College, Cambridge.

Only at the Freie Universitat Berlin after 168 could an essentially sane at historian have hit upon the idea of recasting his former research on the royal gold of the Holy Roman Empire in the bourgeois brass and proletarian iron of mass production in Wilhelmine Germany. However, the "mania for gadgets" of which Rathenau speaks had already been imparted to me by my teachers Hermann Schnitzler and Erich Meyer and my friend Hans Swarzenski. The book developed out of a seminar held in the Department of Art History at the Freie Universitat Berlin in the winter of 197273; the book's constributors also came from this seminar lexcept for Fritz Neumeyer, from the Technische Universitat Berlin).

Credit for the homogeneity of this account of the AEG-Behrens project is due to Henning Rogge, who in over three years of joint work helped to piece the text together out of a series of disorganized lecture notes, seminar papers, and occasional contributions to Festschriften. In addition to making contributions to the text, Gabriele Heidecker and Karin Wilhelm played a major part in the creation of the book, and Sabine Bohle and Fritz Neumeyer willingly offered the relevant sections of their own dissertations. By setting a date for the exhibition, François Burkhardt also set one for the book. Heinz Peters, our German publisher, supervised its realization with an enthusiasm that enabled him to visualize the end product before even a single line had been written. He also dealt nobly with the difficulties of working with several authors at once. My secretary in Berlin, Veronika von Below, ensured that my research was given strict priority, and, together with Renate Klotz, my Bonn secretary, typed the many versions of the manuscript.

For their help, information, and references, we authors thank Ernst Busche, Berlin; Thomas Deecke, Berlin; Hans Fürstenberg, Geneva; Princess Margaret of Hesse and Rhein, Wolfsgarten; Hans-Jürgen Imiela, Mainz; Ulrich Kerkhoff, Bonn; Christiane and Jürgen Klebs, Berlin; Jürgen Krause, Berln; Marie-Agnes von Puttkamer, Bonn; Gert Schiff, New York; Carl Schmitt,

Plettenberg; Ernst Schulin, Freiburg; Messrs. Bandzia, Drinkel, and Galtz, Kraftwerk Unian, Berlin; Messrs. Neumann, Obst, and Schädel, AEG-Telefunken, Brunnenstrasse, Berlin; Mr. Salbach, Berlin, wha after 1925 was respansible for the outstanding exhibitians maunted by the AEG; Mr. Mankawius, BEWAG, Berlin; Mr. Matschke, HEW Museum, Hamburg; Bodo Herzag, Gutehaffnungshütte Archive, Oberhausen; Klaus Popitz, Kunstbibliathek, Stiftung Preussischer Kulturbesitz, Berlin; Wilhelm Weber, Pfalzgalerie, Kaiserslautern; J. H. Müller and A. C. Funk, Karl Ernst Osthaus Museum, Hagen; Messrs. Berninger and Heitmeier, Deutsches Museum, Munich; the Periadicals Department af the Deutsche Staatsbibliothek, East Berl; the Institut r Zeitungsforschung, Dortmund; the Klingspar Museum, Offenbach; and the Kaiser Wilhelm Museum, Krefeld.

I dedicatey share his bok y wie Daphne Buddensg.

ESSAYS

# Industriekultur: Peter Behrens and the AEG, 1907-1914

Tilmann Buddensieg

Our compulsion to formulate concepts, categories, aims, and laws should not be understood as something that enables us to define the real world, but rather os a compulsion to create a world in which our existence becomes possible.   
Friedrich Nietzsche' Inorder to make a lucid, logical, and clearly articulated entity out of an arc lamp, . . . a complete transformotion of our oesthetic notions was necessary.   
Poul Westheim²2

## Peter Behrens and Paul Jordan: The 1907 Program and Its Consequences

On 28 July 1907 the Berliner Tageblatt announced on its arts page that the director of the Kunstgewerbeschule in Düsseldorf, Professor Peter Behrens, who was well known for his outstanding work in the applied arts, had ogreed to take up an appointment in Berlin as artistic odviser to the Allgemeine Elektricitots-Gesellschaft. This was followed on 29 August by a specially commissioned account entitled "Kunst in der Techik," in which Behrens described his plans and the AEG's willingness to adopt them.

Even before Behrens's appointment, some of the AEG's new products had been admired for the technical qualities of their designs; their external form, however, had been left entirely to the toste of the individual Werkmeister. This random method was now to be replaced by o new manner of design that, "following the tendency of the age," would allow for "the most intimate union possible between art and industry," and would renounce "the imitation of handcraftsmanship, of other materials, and of historical styles." The aim was to arrive at "those forms that derive directly fram and which correspand to the machine and machine praduction." This could be achieved, said Behrens, by "concentrating on the exact implementation af the techniques of mechanical production." He then - gestd that inhe desiga lamps electric n sitgear hes, ccuit breakers contact, and the like t would be vald by using stanard types, to "strive after a graceful beauty [anmutige Schönheit] . . . clearly canstructed and appropriate to the materials used." The graceful beauty appropriate ta typical products af industrial mass production should be achieved, he said, without recaurse to historicism or to the stylistic individualism that had been demanded af the artist by Jugendstil. Such a manner of design would resolve the conflict between the technical character of electrical appliances and the "artistic layout of a room'" in favar of an "all-embracing artistic order." Consistent with the "logical application of these intentions," the AEG would alsa "attach great importance ta the artistic and typographic design of all its publicatians" and ta the design of its future exhibitions. Behrens concluded the article with a laak into a future in which applied art would inevitably be industrially praduced. Thus, "the practical realization of these ideas" would alsa make possible "the conversion of artistic and creative work into material assets," and the applied arts would gain an ecanomic and social function.

In contrast ta the fine arts, which exist "independent of public life," Behrens saw the applied arts as a public utility far the use af society at large.³ This "intimate union" between the techniques and farms af mass production and "artistic and creative work" wauld ga beyond the interest of a few, would prepare the way for "a general improvement in public taste," and would enable art to penetrate into "the mast distant strata of the papulatian." To the concepts of public taste and art, Behrens added propriety (Anstand). His wark for the AEG would help, he felt, to spread this concept throughout all sectians of saciety. By propriety he first meant fitness ar aptness of design and constructian. At the same time, however, the word alsa had a more elevated sense and referred to the educational and instructive powers of beautiful, gracefully formed objects.

From these principles it can be seen that, from the outset, Behrens and the AEG saw their collaboratian nat simply as the work of a new department of product design but as something of more fundamental impartance. The cansiderable interest Behrens's appointment excited in the press attested to the great hopes that were pinned on the collaboration between "one of our leading artists and one of the world's largest companies."4 These hopes were to find a national forum in the Deutscher Werkbund, which was founded soon after with basic aims the same as thase Behrens had outlined.

Behrens's work far the AEG began with the relatively unimportant task of redesigning the company journal. He then turned his attention to the arc lamp, and from that he went on to establish an all-embracing AEG style that extended ta the design of factories, transport depots, and model housing estates for the firm's employees. In response ta Behrens's work, the AEG's board of directors cautiously extended his contract step by step into what was prabably the widest-ranging commission of modern times.

Although the projects Behrens had completed by 1907 were well known and had been dacumented in the professional literature, they were not very numerous. The list included his own hause in the artists'colony at Darmstadt (1900), successful but temporary pavilions at exhibitions held at Turin (1902), St. Louis (1904), Cologne (1906), and Dresden (1906), and half a dozen smaller commissions in Saarbrücken, Wetter an der Ruhr, and Hagen. The formal consistency, intellectual rigor, and universal applicability of these works, coupled with Behrens's outstanding ability as product designer, graphic artist, and architect, must have commended him to the directors of the AEG.

Because of wartime losses, the exact circumstances and considerations that led to Behrens's appointment can no longer be reconstructed from the archives. Behrens himself said that Paul Jordan, the director responsible for the AEG's factories, had initiated the appointment,5 and this has been confirmed by other sources. Jordan was one of the oldest and closest colleagues of Emil Rathenau, the founder of the AEG. The sure judgment the firm displayed from the outset in employing outstanding architects, designers, and even sculptors can be attributed to Jordan. With men like Emil and Walther Rathenau, Carl Fürstenberg, and Felix Deutsch connected with the AEG, expert knowledge and judgment in artistic matters was ensured.

The engagement of Behrens must be seen in the context of the AEG's history. Franz Schwechten (1841-1921), the designer of the Anhalter Bahnhof (1875), was one of Berlin's leading architects. For the AEG he built the factory on the Ackerstrasse (completed by 1889) and the monumental portal on the Brunnenstrasse (1896). Schwechten was also responsible for the buildings of the Berliner Elektrizitatswerke on the Voltastrasse (1899) and, indirectly, for other AEG factories built in his style by assistants before the arrival of Behrens.

As a result of his pioneering Wertheim store (1896), Alfred Messel emerged in the late 1890s as Berlin's most prominent architect. After holding an unsuccessful competition, the AEG commissioned him to design a new administration building for the Friedrich-Karl-Ufer. In 1906, however, the prospect of further collaboration between Messel and the AEG was ruled out by his involvement with a major building project on the "Museumsinsel" in Berlin, which fully occupied him until his death in 1909.

The daring appointment of Otto Eckmann to design the AEG's catalogs and other printed material for the 1900 World Exhibition in Paris was also a brief episode, for Eckmann died in 1902. Nevertheless, his appointment confirmed the AEG's intention to entrust the design of its publicity material to outstanding artists using modern techniques, rather than follow the academic taste of the Wilhelmine court or that of the company's principal customer, the general public. Among companies of a comparable size, only the AEG had such an adventurous attitude toward design. However, neither the printed materials nor the products designed between the death of Otto Eckmann in 1902 and the arrival of Behrens in 1906 show any trace of an original hand.

An important part in the AEG's search for a unifying force was played by the Dritte Deutsche Kunstgewerbeausstellung (Third German Exhibition of Applied Arts) held in Dresden in 1906. For this exhibition, Behrens produced a highly formalized design for a new type of display pavilion (illustration 2) for the Anker Linoleum Company. Behrens developed this design from a more modest one he had produced for an earlier exhibition in Oldenburg.8 The Anker Company also commissioned Behrens to design most of its patterns, its trademark, and its advertisement on the back cover of the exhibition catalog. The formal, unified image set Anker apart from the other exhibitors and must have offered a useful starting point for those people in the AEG who were responsible for the reorganization of that firm's design policy.

At the time of the Dresden exhibition, the AEG was, without doubt, already considering its contributions to theElekrotechnik in der Kleinndustrie exhibition planned for the summer of 1907 and the Berliner Schiffbau-Ausstellung to be held in the summer of 1908. Behrens's pavilion at the latter was his first major project for the AEG, and a comparison with the Anker pavilion reveals that the decision to appoint Behrens had its roots at Dresden. Both of these pavilions were free-standing buildings in the classical manner, physically and stylistically removed from the other, rather conventional exhibition halls.

Paul Jordan seems to have been convinced by the conclusions drawn by Ernst Schur in his critique of Behrens's contribution to the Dresden exibi-B l present time; he stimulates industry by working with it.

The press was unanimous in its approval of Behrens's explanatory manifesto, of his first, modest efforts, and of his increasingly comprehensive work for the AEG. From the wide range of contemporary comments, here aevwo he monfl critAt e be 1908, Joseph August Lux wrote that the "enfeebled product design" of firms like the AEG should be replaced by "typical and consistent forms derived from the practical demands made by the products themselves." Thhe ai was t  ask f thecnuy. ar Ernst t banker's son and a patron of the arts, celebrated the union of industry and art that economic necessity had brought about. He called it "a triumph that exceeds even the most daring hopes of recent years," and added that "art has once again gained public acceptance in Germany.'" Two years later, a visit to the turbine factory confirmed Osthaus's enthusiastic assessment: "That which the Deutscher Werkbund has recently been striving for with great energy and gradually increasing success has been accomplished by the AEG with one bold decision: The AEG has given back to the world its right to art in the industrial process."12 Karl Scheffler, who from the outset had vigorously supported Behrens's work for the AEG in his influential journal Kunst und Künstler, described the "new style" Behrens had created for the AEG as the "style of the approaching epoch of world trade.'13

Comments like these emphasized the change in the "spirit of cammercial enterprise," which Scheffler said had now been made aware of "its sblilthg asi  qui Berens,  certainly refleccontporary eeng.His work haver, wos not merely a response to the specific problems of the period; it also pontd the woy forward. The progressive, specifically twentieth-century qualities are not difficult to identify.

Behrens transformed the cancept of art. In describing the "Industriekunst of the future, he pronounced the end of industrially produced "art" in the sense of the mechanical copying of traditional ornament, moterials, and craftsmanship. Insteod of forcing new, mechonical oppliances to conform to the formal and decorotive norms of their hand-built precursors, Behrens sought to give them a form appropriate to their nature and to the means of their production. With Behrens's AEG program clearly in mind, Joseph August Lux summarized these ideas in Die neue Zeit, the weekly magazine of the German Social Democrotic Porty:

Works of art cannot be manufoctured and duplicated en masse. The task of industry is not to produce art, . . . but rather ta promote good taste. The appointment of outstanding artists to leading positions in industrial companies is of great importance to the general improvement of public taste. Practicol considerations demand that the artist wha works for industry should produce unodorned, correct designs that achieve their effect simply through good proportions and an enhanced sense of function. The artists themselves would be the last to claim that these designs and their industriol production were a contribution to the arts. They should merely satisfy the demonds of cultivated taste. For this reason, it is essentiol today to employ artists, and, what is more, the best artists. It is agreed, however, that the true roots of art are not to be found here.'5

This thaughtful description of the novel tasks confronting the "artistic advisers" ta industry and the new responsibilities that had been thrust on industry by econamic necessity was written by Lux in 1909. At this early date he defined an explosive contradiction: Only the very best artists could design industrial products in such a way that they would have nothing to do with art. Using nothing but "unadorned, correct designs," the artists cauld find an effective role as purveyars of good taste. At the mme y couee oeal atnhear their work was no longer categorized as art.

However, artists who did not seek their work in the way Lux did viewed these new developments with great hostility.' They were now engaged on two fronts: against the customary incomprehension of the public, and against artists who designed for mass production (who were pleased that their work had a social function and that their products could not be works of art, and who were delighted thot art wos to be found "somewhere quite different").

The appointment of Behrens by Jordan provided a test case. Through the allionce of art and technology, it appeared that the social isolation of the artist had been done away with and thot the industrial mass production of trash would be replaced by the large-scale manufacture of well-designed goods. The aim was to bring together the apparently irrecancilable spheres of art and design, technology and commerce, machine production, and economic viability to their individual and mutual advantage, and for the public good. Through the example of practical art—through a "reorganizatian of everything visible"17—the aesthetic pleasure of the individual in an exclusive "fine art" was to be tronsformed and extended into a cultural mission for mass education. This was not merely the utopian dream of one man, but a project canceived in conjunction with an industrial giant, with all its economic power and potential for social penetration—"only a great industrial enterprise" could "alter the general condition of society in favor of culture.'8

By no meons did the charisma of this model for the praductian and the sale of pragress depend entirely on its obility to pramate better taste. Its attroctian went much further, surmaunting oll politicol controdictions. An ideol process con be defined: Platanicolly beoutiful, good ond useful objects would be moss-produced by contented workers in well-designed foctories. Nat anly would these abjects be beoutiful ond useful in themselves; they would olso be on advonced expression af the industrial oge ond would be sold in well-designed stores and shops, which would oct as suppliers of culture and good toste ta all levels of saciety.Depending on the palitical viewpoint of the interpreter, this process would promote saciolism, or the spread af democrocy, or the creotion of the style af the oge, or a strengthening of the economic competitiveness af the natian. Whot other praductive element in Wilhelmine society could cloim oll this?

It is o common error in ort-histarical palemics to describe this concept in sweeping terms os bourgeois, or os the product of industriol copitolism. Hawever, recent reseorch into interest ond pressure groups in the Wilhelmine period hos shown thot the lorge firms thot emborked on madernizotion progroms were ottocked from oll sides. Although individually powerful, these firms represented only o smoll port of monufacturing industry. They were ottocked not only by commerciol argonizatians representing smaller firms thot were not members of the Werkbund, but alsa by afficial industriol ond commerciol ogencies.

In gloring controst to Behrens's AEG program and ta the aims of the Werkbund, the powerful Valkskunst ond Heimotkunst movements sow only "the greatest controdiction between art ond business . . . between the simple pursuit af beouty ond rotionol colculotion." Volkskunst, colled o "fountoin of youth thot would reresh the visuol culureof the notin, couldony survive, it wos claimed, under o "noncopitolist, semisociolist, cooperotive system."21

The faundation of the Werkbund in Octaber 1907 had been stimulated by dissotisfoction with the Fochverbond für die wirtschoftlichen Interessen des Kunstgewerbes (Assaciation for the Economic Interests of the Applied Arts), which, predictably enough, become one of the Werkbund's most vigaraus appanent groups. At its third congress in June 1909, the Fochverband launched o sovoge attock agoinst "modern tendencies in art." Director Behr observed in his speech, and nat without some justificatian, thot "the mojority of housing schemes adhere ta traditional farms," thot "modern forms ore fovored only when funds ore limited," ond thot "by odvocoting simplicity in its progrom, modernism hos become the style of those of moderote means." Cleorly, Behr looked down on the allionce between the ortist and "those of moderote meons." At the 1900 Poris Exhibition, Otta Eckmonn hod viewed the "expensive, bockword-looking junk" on display with equol contempt, comporing it with the things designed by his ortist friends in Munich, which were "cheop ond took their forms from the new ort."

By moking his lamps ond kettles simple and undecarated, Peter Behrens freed these opplionces from the saciol-stotus connototions of expensive exclusivity ond from the ornomentotion oppropriote to these connototions. Using calor and form os his oesthetic meons, he opened the woy for domestic use ond moss consumption of electric mochines, tools, switches, fans, ond kitchen equipment. With hisoriginolity ond lock of compromise, Behrens shoped o completely new sensibility ond receptivity toword design thot tronscended mere utility ond such stotus-bound notions os good toste ar possession. "Simple form" os o "cheop" style for those of "moderote meons" meont in reolity thot the civilizing ottributes of technology were mode avoiloble to new sections of the populotion. (Enaugh hos olreody been written obout the negotive consequences of this optimistic storting position.

![](images/00d64a9fce03af58558d172ec5f7ed80e86f4f3473fe02f511b5ce1936d9761f.jpg)  
Poster for electrical heating and cooking appliances. AEG photo 8149, 15 September 1911.

Once again, we come across the correlation between technological development, an awakening by businessmen to market needs, and "simple form." It was entiey stifablehat the nreed prouction an dtion of more useful and more attractive goods should, at the same time, have been registered by salesmen as an increase in turnover, described by the engineer as technical progress, and viewed by artists and art critics as a manifestation of the inevitable trend toward democratization.23 The AEG concpt was thus engaged at several levels in the contpoay debaten industrial and commercial policy.

Were there an architectural study of the administration buildings and production plants of the members of the industrial interest groups in the Wilhelmine period, then the fundamental contradictions of their architectural images would be very clear. On one side was manufacturing industry, export-intensive and working with "surrogate" materials. On the other side were the members of the "Bund von Eisen, Kohle und Garn mit Getreide" (Union of Iron, Coal, Yarn, and Grain). (This contrast can only be mentioned here as an unresearched problem.)

## 4

Thus, in simplified terms, the Behrens-AEG experiment was an economically vital union between the work of an artist, which reached all social levels and promoted turnover, and the products of an export-intensive branch of manufacturing industry: The experiment drew fire from all sides—it was criticized by the advocates of "semisocialist" Volkskunst, by the reactionaries in the Heimatkunst movement, by the supporters of arts and crafts and of "fine art," by heavy industry and agriculture, and by the majority of the Social Democrats. The place of the artist in this process was the subject of a furious argument at the celebrated 1914 Werkbund conference at Cologne.25

During the wor, Hermonn Muthesius returned to the thoughts on the relotionship between ort ond the mochine which he hod first formuloted in 1902,26 ond which, in the meontime, hod been odopted by Behrens. In 1917 Muthesius described the essence of moss production os the seriol repetition of obsolutely consistent single objects. He sow moss production os the industriol form of the future, os it reflected the contemporory trend of both products ond men toword ononymity-—o trend he welcomed without reservotion.

A sensitive point in the history of the relotionship between ort ond technology wos thus reoched. Behrens's originol intention in 1907 hod been to emoncipote the individuol from the formless, onorchic moteriolism of monufocturing industry by creoting on otmosphere in which good design could flourish. This implied o complete visuol reorgonizotion involving the firm's products, production processes, working conditions, ond housing. All of this wos tronsformed by Muthesius's definition from o bourgeois liberating impulse into the exoct opposite. Werner Sombort commented ostutely thot "while living proctitioners in the orts were ottempting, by meons of good design, to free themselves from the deod motter thot threotened to overwhelm them, . . . in the sphere of science, moteriol concerns were increosingly subjugoting the humon element."27 With the spreod of stondordizotion ond ononymity in the world of technology ond industry, the meons were developed for o new subjugotion of the individuol. This hod been the reol theme of the Werkbund debote in 1914.28 By the outbreok of the October Revolution, the Russion Constructivists hod derived from it the methodology for their ortistic offensive. In controst, the bourgeois ortists in the Werkbund roised the cry "Bock to ort os croftsmonship!" ot their Stuttgort conference in 1919. In his Stuttgort oddress, entitled "The Tosks of the Werkbund," Hons Poelzig renounced the olionce between ort ond industry. Industry, he soid, wos concerned "simply with technicol motters" ond wos "ruled by economic considerotions." Art, however, wos o "wil to form," which should discloim ony connection with business. Only then, felt Poelzig, could ort defend itself ogoinst foshionoble exploitotion by moss production ond ogoinst stondordizotion. The Werkbund, Poelzig on "technology ond industry."29

Behrens sensed clearly thot the very essence of his work for the AEG wos being chollenged. In 1920 he wos forced to ocknowledge "the collopse of o technicolly ond economicolly odvonced civilizotion," ond he bemooned the spreod of "despoiring resignotion" ond "the conscious destruction f form. "n  ploce,"he soi, "opeor morphous creotis, the deformotion of noturol forms, dishormony ond dissononce." Even before the wor o "new oge" hod been prophesied in Expressionist ort.30 This highly subjective ort wos denounced ot the time by the procticolminded Behrens os hoving no beoring on the reolity of the time. His own oim, os o procticol ortist, hod been to "ennoble" the technologicol civilizotion ond to "infuse it with spirituol life." Confronted by the ruins of this civilizotion, he wos now forced to odmit thot Expressionism hod, ofter oll, been the true monifestotion of the oge.

Behrens begon to work on his design for the Memoriol Holl in the odministrotion building of l.G.-Forben ot Höchst illustrotion 5) in the outumn of 1920.3 The controst between the rodicolly new style of the Höchst complex ond, soy, the odministrotion building Behrens hod designed for the AEG in 1917 (illustrotion A163 in cotolog below) must be understood in terms of his troumotic reolizotion.

Other influences, too, were of work. After the mechonized sloughter of the wor, Behrens sought ot Höchst to purge both industry ond himself by invoking the spirituol help of Gothic mysticism ond of Goethe's Forbenlehre. The Höchst complex stonds in complete controst to the optimistic rotionolism of the offices Behrens hod designed before the wor for Monnesmonn ond for Continentol. Equolly striking is the ronge of expressive power invested in the design of on office. In on essoy he wrote in 1920

Peter Behrens, IG-Farben factary at Hächst, charcaal drawing, 1926. Courtesy af Princess Margaret van Hessen und bei Rhein, Schlass Wolfsgarten.

![](images/b351947ee56923e8072e17321bcbee17ff391e07606fa7af7e9f90b4d96500e9.jpg)

for Hermann Graf Keyserling's Schule der Weisheit (School of Wisdom) at Darmstadt, Behrens described the essence of this architecture: "The building laws are mysterious. Multiplicity expands into the infinite and incalculaimposing building masses, but seems to be overcome entirely. Space does not enclose, does not confine, it leads into incalculable depths, climbs into distant heights."

Max Brod had already recognized this reversal of opinion when he commented in April 1914 that the application of rational and seeingly rational measures has been so terribly overrated that we want now in our bad conscience to sate our spirits with a gluttonous orgy of irrationalism."32

In the immediate postwar years, Behrens, like Poelzig, Gropius, and Taut, believed that art had to return to its roots in craftsmanship. Yet by 1922 he was already looking for a way out of the seemingly inevitable schism between the worlds of art and technology. Behrens admitted that "at present the intellectuals in particular" viewed technology as "the expression of a calculating, analytical, capitalist and imperialist age." They desired, he said, by means of an art based on spiritualization and emotional simplicity," to "escape back to distant lands and past times." He pointed to the schism that prevented "both the engineer and the man of artistic sensibility from taking part in all aspects of our worldly existence." "Yet no one," he said, "could seriously dispense with the benefits of modern technology." Of these benefits he went on to say: "We need them today more than ever, and in the best and cheapest form. Precisely because we are economically depressed, we must make our life simpler, more practical, and more comprehensible. Only with the aid of industry can this be achieved. But those who do not want to have their life split between spirituality and technology are also right. Whether or not technology can succeed in becoming a means to and an expression of culture rather than remaining an end in itelf is therefore a question of great historical importance."33 Behrens thereby redefined the original problem of 1907.

Behrens's pupil Walter Grapius conceived the Bauhaus as a laboratory in which this problem wauld be mastered. In 1907 Behrens had felt obliged in his work for the AEG "to bring art and propriety to the most distant strata of the population." With the Bauhaus at Weimar, Gropius now wanted ta reach—in the language of the Expressionists—"new, intellectually untapped layers of the people."34

Inhe spring 14 ina paper given ohe Design and Industris Association in London, Gropius described the aims of the Bauhaus and laid out a number of its main principles:

This conception of the essential unity of all creative work was what inspired me in founding the Bauhaus as a school of design. In carrying out this scheme I tried to solve the ticklish problem of combining imagination with technical proficiency, and strave to train a new and hitherto nonexistent type of collaborator who should be equally skilled in design and technical proficiency. . . . The Bauhaus accepted the machine as the essentialy modern vehicle of form, and sought to come to terms with it. Its workshops were really laboratories in which practical designs for presentday goods were conscientiously worked out as models for mass production, and were continually being improved on. This dominant aim of creating typeforms to meet every commercial, technical, and aesthetic requirement necessitated a picked body af men of all-around culture who were tharoughly experienced in the practical and mechanical as well as the theoretical, scientific, and formal aspects of design, and who were well versed in the laws on which these are based. The constructors of these madels had alsa to be fully acquainted with factory methods of mechanical mass production, which are radically different from those of handicraft, although the variaus parts of the prototypes they evolved had naturally to be made by hand. It is from the individual peculiarities of every type of machine that the new but stll individual "genuineness" and "beauty" of its praducts are derived, whereas illogical machine imitation of handmade goods infallibly bears the stamp of a makeshift substitute.

The Bauhaus represented the school of thought that believes that the difference between industry and handicraft is far less a difference due to the nature of the tools employed than of the effect of subdivision of labor in the former and one-man control from start to finish in the latter. Handicrafts and industry must, however, be understood as opposites perpetually approaching each other. Handicrafts are now changing their traditional nature. In future their field will be in research work for industrial production and in speculative experiments in laboratory-workshops where the preparatory work of evolving and perfecting new typeforms wil be done.

. . . Our guiding principle was that artistic design is neither an intellectual nor a material affair, but simply an integral part of the very stuff of life. Further, the revolution in artistic mentality has brought in its train that new elementary knowledge which is implied in the new conception of design, in the same way that the technical transformation of industry has provided new tools for its realization. Our object was to permeate both types of mind; to liberate the creative artist from his otherworldliness and reintegrate him into the workaday world of realities; and at the same time to broaden and and humanize the rigid, almost exclusively material mind of the businessman. Our governing conception of the basic unity of all design in its relation to life, which informed all our work, was therefore in diametrical opposition to that of "art for art's sake" and the even more dangerous philosophy it sprang from: business as an end in itself.35

It is clear that after 1918 Gropius's ideas had freed themselves from the philanthropic and nationalistic tendencies af Friedrich Naumann, which had inspired both Behrens and Gropius prior ta 1914. The Gropius of the Bauhaus had also lost his faith in reform from above, to be brought about gradually by a handful of enlightened industrialists and artists working together in a nonpolitical pressure group like the Werkbund. Also missing was Walther Rathenau's belief in the ability of the capitalist system, when developed to its full potential, to eliminate poverty, alienation, and educational deprivation and to overcome production deficits and unprofitability.36

Unlike Behrens, Grapius and the Bauhaus were not attached ta any firm. Gropius's work, therefore, had na advertising or publicity functian. Furthermare, he did not have to demanstrate the validity af his designs through the praducts af a world-renawned firm in apen competition with other companies. Grapius was ultimately able to see the wark of his "laborataryas that of a team af teachers and students, whereas Behrens' tdia wasganized ina highly efficient manner. Althaugh Behrensvy wisely kept his independence by reserving his right to work for ather firms, he was nevertheless always concerned with practical commissions. He did not have the freedam enjayed by the Bauhaus, which, as a relatively independent agency, cauld choose prajects that would provide both pratatypes far possible industrial praductian and teaching materials far a schaol of art and design.

To acknawledge the presence of Behrens's 1907 program in Gropius's 1934 paper in na way detracts fram the histarical impartance af the Bauhaus. On the contrary, it reveals a happy and (in Germany) rare example af lang-term develapment and continuity. In both cases one can detect the passion for the "perfectian of material achievement' that had moved Max Weber, an seeing the designs af Behrens, Olbrich, and Pankok at the 1904 St. Lauis Warld's Fair, to feel "praud of his awn nation." These new German products prampted even in Weber the momentary thought that in the design af pots, glasses, chairs, and househald utensils the Germans would be "the mentars of the West. "37

Gropius's ideas provided the basis for Herbert Read's boak Art and Industry (landon, 1934). Read included the extract from Grapius's London lecture quated abave, and added "I have na ather desire in this book than to suppart and prapagate the ideals thus expressed by Dr. Gropius. . . . " As recently as 1976, Nael Carringtan called Read's book "a New Testament for the whale design movement [in Great Britain]." Read's quasi-religiaus language, faith in the "master," and apostolic sense of mission ta spread the "dactrine" all suggest a religious simile with respect ta the Bauhaus: Grapius staod an the broad shaulders of Behrens as the apastles stood on the shaulders of the prophets.

Herbert Read came across the emigrant Gropius in Landon in 1934. Grop was th be ehori Gay begun themselves with the creatian af a new "people's order . . . based on the discrimination between friend and foe, between those who conform to the peaple's arder and those who are alien ta it." Bath Grapius and Mies fell into the alien and un-German categary. In 1935, with the tobacco factary at Linz, Behrens composed the German swan song to architectural internationalism.

It is one af the most oppressive ironies in the history af German art that the uniquely universal impact and influence enjoyed by the two Behrens pupils, Gropius and Mies, was the result of the declaration by the German government that their wark was alien ta the German natian. The model for a madern existence which Naumann, Muthesius, and Behrens saught ta pramate and sell an the pre-1914 world market against bitter international competition now crossed the national frontiers effortlessly. Just at the time when this model was establishing itself as a universal precept and pattern, the members of the Bauhaus, wha were the successors to Naumann, Muthesius, and Behrens, were rabbed of their national identity and called enemies of the peaple.

Behrens and Messel: From the Mythology of Industry to "Art in the Industrial Process'38

. . . the true lover lof art] sees not only the truth in the imitation, but also the virtue of selection and the ingenuity of combination. Goethe39

After January 1894, and possibly even earlier, the AEG used the socalle Goddessof Lightas t rademark ilustration . With flow tresses she sits sideways on a winged wheel. Her right foot rests on a globe, which emerges through the clouds in a profusion of lightning flashes. With her right hand she raises a glowing light bulb into the night sky. Compared with the bulb, the stars and the narrow crescent of the moon give out only a meager glimmer of light.

By adopting the "Goddess of Light," Emil Rathenau followed the widespread tendency of industry to portray its aims, its technological processes, and its dynamic power in mythological terms. In the case of the AEG, which was offering light and power for the common good, the mythological reference—the power of the AEG transcending the light of the heavens—was proud to the point of arrogance. In other cases it was more modest, as in the Carlsberg brewery's attempt to link its beer with Ceres, the goddess of grain. Further examples of industrial mythologizing were offered by the Flohr Elevator Company, whose advertising posters showed Sisyphus rolling his stone up the hill, and the Berliner Elektrizitätswerk (a subsidiary of the AEG), which decorated a commemorative publication in 1897 with a drawing of a dynamo inhabited by a female figure wrapn lutincoe, steam power was created in Hades by giants with long beards; electric power, in contrast, was the province of innocent maidens.

Emil Rathenau would doubtless have agreed with Werner Sombart that "the only motive for economic processes is organized human trading," and that technology should be understood as the functional arrangement omaterial goo r human needs.There was, nevertheless, adee rooted need to defend, comprehend, or extol technology in terms of its mythological and symbolic origins. Sombart's more sober approach prevailed, but only very slowly. In the 1901 lecture cited above, he explained that it was absurd to declare that "steam is the driving force of our modern economic life, as one constantly reads." "Steam, gentlemen," he continued, "is the driving force of the steam engine; that which installs the engine in a particular place and for a particular purpose is the will of the industrialist." But the extent of its power to create and utilize new sources of energy made it hard for industry at that time to identify wit Sombart' functionalist definition Smilarly, the revolution in Germany's social and cultural lie was too obvious, the hopes attached to industry too optimistic, and the criticism of the negative effects of industrialization too menacing for Sombart's formula to gain easy acceptance.

With the advent of Jugendstil, industry's attempt to define its own image entered a second phase, and naturalistic symbolism was abandoned. The production of energy was no longer portrayed as the wonderfully effortless and pure labor of those ubiquitus goddesses who ignited arc lamps, fed power stations, and wove telephone connections out of electric wires. By commissioning Otto Eckmann, a leading Jugendstil artist, to design its contributions to the Paris World Exhibition, the AEG already demonstrated in 1900 that it saw a correspondence between its own work and that of the avant-garde. Among Eckmann's designs for the AEG was a new trademark to replace the one, probably designed by Franz Schwechten, that had first appeared in gold mosaic on the portal on the Brunnnenstrasse (ilustration 1. For Schwechten's three initials, composed of interlocking acanthus tendrils, Eckmann substituted three letters in his own "Eckmannschrift"—one of the first modern typefaces of the twentieth century. In the few graphic designs Eckmann produced for the AEG one can see haw he used his characteristically Jugendstil curves to frame the produ of the irm and thereby invest them with a certain ynamism. This dynamism, however, was Eckmann's own, and bore no specific relation to technalagy. Indeed, it was equally apprapriate an the book jackets af the plays af Gerhart Hauptmann which Eckmann designed for the S. Fischer publishing house in Leipzig. Eckmann's graphic work deserves at best a limited acknawledgement, as proaf that the AEG had turned to a madern artist af his stature. This manner of graphic design was specifically Eckmann's, however. It could not free itself fram the personality of the artist ar create a new and unmistakable style appropriate to an industrial concern such as the AEG.

Illustrations o industrial products, trademarks, and factoris framed byo namental borders originated in early-nineteenth-century England and became an internatianally favared motif in sales catalogs, commemarative publicatians, and advertisements. One af the thousands of possible examples is a catalog published in 1904 by the Westinghause Electric and Manufacturing Company of Pittsburgh (illustratians 9, 10). This differed fram, say, the 1884 catalog of the Chicago firm Van de Paele—which actually shawed the functianal blessings of electricity in a realistic manner (illustration 11)—in much the same way as Eckmann's work differed from its predecessars. Exactly a thousand years after floriate decoration had been used in the liturgical baoks of the tenth century, it enjoyed a remarkable revival in the age of electrical mythology. Instead af illuminating the sanctus ar te igitur in manuscripts such as Saint Gal Psalters, it was now wound around the initials af industrial firms. Rather than rely simply an photagraphs, the catalogs depicted electrical power with an ornamental and quasi-religiaus symbolism that invoked "the sentimentalism of the allegory of power."41
