af time gradually but clearly evalved away fram a sharp rectangularity toward a safter and more rounded profile. (Campare the trademark in illustratians G79 and G80 with the names af the manths in illustrations G20-G22.) Same af these variations can be attributed ta the accasian and the nature af the publication; far example, a more static alignment af the lettering was used in the official announcement shown in illustration G11, whereas a more individual style, with the cantraction of A and E and with ablique seris, was used a characterize the AEG's awn catala ta the 1908 Schiffbau-Ausstellung (illustration A1).

With his upper-case script, Behrens saught to integrate into ane version all the current variations on the classical Roman script. The result was a flexible script whose weight and rhythm could be elegantly adapted to suit the particular task. For example, in 1907 Behrens redesigned the title banner af the AEG-Zeitung (illustration G7) using an expansive, relaxed script that carresponded to the curvaceous form af the initials. Similarly, the blocklike letters on the cover of the 1907 Mitteilungen der Berliner Elektricitäts-Werke lillustration G2) were compressed together and gaverned by the rigarous structure af this campasitian. The many designs by Behrens and his calleagues shaw haw much a line af print is able ta respand ta the given prapartians withaut lasing its typical characteristics. As a vigaraus handwritten script, the Behrens Raman was chasen wherever an official ar farmal design was needed far a caver ar a similar purpase. These covers were generally printed an calared, heavy paper boards ta heighten the impressian af value and craftsmanship.52

The initial D in the 1907 Rathenau address (illustration G24) and that in the special editian af the AEG-Zeitung published an 11 December 1908 an the occasian af Emil Rathenau's seventieth birthday (ilustration G8) mark the beginning and the end af the development of a calligraphic script inta the AEG's ananymaus hause typescript, Behrens Roman.53 Ther  pi idence gest that Behrens had worke an this script at an earlier date: the poster to the 1906 Deutsche JahrhunertAusstellungBe illustratian.Thi postecarrelonga, rather heavy capital script. Althaugh the serifs were alltriangular, this script can be counted as a farerunner to Behrens Roman. This is made clear by the unusual farm af the N, with twa heavy vertical strokes of the same width and an unstressed diagonal. The most striking characteristics of Behrens Roman are the absalutely vertical ductus and the use of two difeent sr.he near  be wih the pontathe end by being drawn aut of the downstroke toward the right. This gives a sense of progression, especially to the larger letters. The exact, triangular serifs on (for example) the harizantal strokes of E and T reinforce the angular appearance af the script, and this angularity is not affected by the rather ornate hooks on G, W, V, and C. Only the Q dances out of line on its curving tail stroke.

Same of Behrens's cantemporaries were upset by the "steely, rectangular, rigid" quality of the letters.6 Although a "concrete relationship" can be noted between the script and the world af "materials and technol-ac has been described as "nathing ather than two-dimensional architecture,"8 and it is clear that there was a conscious relationship between Behrens's manumental lettering and his architecture, which was daminated at the time by the elongated rectangle, the square, and the circle and which was determined ever more strongly by the principle of the rhythmic sequence. The strong architectonic interdependence of the individual farms and the varying width of the large letters, which were further enriched by an additional raund E, made possible an "unfettered rhythm" at the expense af "the earlier stylistic demand far regularity in the dimensians af each letter."59 This was in accordance with Behrens's conception af rhythm as the essence of the modern age. It followed that this rhythmic quality shauld also find expression in a typeface; "after architecture," Behrens said, print should give "the most characteristic picture of an age."o He did not, however, follaw Larisch's recommendation that the spaces between letters be constant. The alternatian of circles with selfcontained rectangular farms points to an architectural matif which belongs as a decorative element to the Behrens Roman script: the abstracted frieze of astragal or triglyph and metope (illustration 15).

15 Bere, ca, Hn 190607.  
![](images/04610fb505d6f630860df6376d457eac39685a1e7931e1c7489609e3e790fa0f.jpg)

Unlike Wiliam Morris, who based his Golden Type on "the Roman script of the Italian Renaissance," Behrens began his studies with the ninth-century Carolingian capital and uncial script.6 (This script was modeled on Roman inscriptions, and also used the "early medieval semi-uncial."63) By B expressed in the Roman lapidary script with the "Germanic cultural sphere." According to Rodenberg, Behrens's direct model was the fifthcentury Codex Argenteus in Upsala. "The uncial model," Rodenberg noted, "can be seen in the capitals D, F, P, U, and the round E."64 The particular beginning to the hair stroke and the form of the N was also aken from the old scripts." The introductory brochure to the Behrens Roman cript (ee otsid the oll:

The similarity in character between this Roman script and the early-medieval semi-uncial and Caralingian script shows the lasting power of a genuine tradition. This is still the mark of a mature artistic achievement.

Out of this tradition developed the search for a rhythmical and lively script form based on the succession of narrower and broader capital letters: the narrow P and R, the broad G, O, and D. In contrast to the preference in the scripts of recent years for uniform, equally weighted capitals and regular spacing, a well-thought-out and rhythmical alteration in the spacing is, without doubt, an artistic enrichment.

It is to be hoped that, in its imitation of the Roman script of the Germanic culture, the Behrens script wll assist the task of reviving the Roman script in the German spirit . . . and will point to a solution. . . . let us hope that a further valuable example will be added to this homogeneous and German graphic style, let us hope that the foundation has been laid for a DEUTSCHE ANTIQUA [German Roman script]!"

The Behrens Roman script has been described as "the maturest prod-a the calaphycurganize  Beens s ec Düsseldorf Kunstgewerbeschule), Anna Simons, and F. H. Ehmcke on behalf of the Prussian Ministry of Commerce and offered to teachers at colleges of art and technology.

At the Paris fair of 1900, the individualistic style of Eckmann's AEG publicity represented an avant-garde departure from the more conventional form of the official catalog.67 Now, at the 1910 Brussels World's Fair, everything in the official German catalog, including the individual advertisements of the various exhibiting firms, was printed in Behrens Roman, and the script originally designed for the AEG was displayed as a typeface appropriate for the new industrial Germany. Not only did the "Behrens style" establish itself as identical with the "AEG style," but the "AEG style" was also the "national style" of typography. The unique quality af this style was itabiliy tounite Geranic and classical elements in a script of modern proportions and impassive clarity. The development from the original monogram of the firm into the abstract AEG trademark was a search for graphic anonymity, conducted within the bounds of the firm. The circular signet of 1907 (illustration G77) was very similar to the ER monogram which Behrens stamped inta the binding af the Rathenau address of the same year (ilustration G23). It is probable that these last vestiges of a personal handwriting were consciously applied to the trademark to emphasize the personal relationship between the founder and the firm. A stylistic unity was created between Emil Rathenau's monogram and that of the AEG.69 The aval signet of 1908 (illustration G78) also had this monogram character, but did not suggest the same sense of dignity.

With the hexagonal symbol shown in illustrations 1 and G79, the AEG trademark was launched on ts publicity campaign. Each item produced by the firm carried the same stamp. According to Wolf Dohrn,70 it was "a symbol of the prevailng spir  labor"and was used enthusiastically by the Werkmeister, who "saw the new mark as the true symbol of their work."The development of an entirely abstract symbol—three letters in a rectangular frame (illustration G80)—was to follow. The rectangularframed version, which first appeared in 1912, conformed to the general trend toward a softer contour, which was also evident in the Behrens Roman script. But something else was different, too. In the hexagon, as in the script, the emphasis had been vertical, with elongated, sharp-edged letters. Suddenly the horizontal axis was dominant, and the letters no longer strained upward but were heavy and squat. Lanzke (1958) felt that specific reasons had also led Bernhard, Hohlwein, and other graphic artists to "heavy lettering, suitable for poster design." For this reason, the AEG trademark that is stillin use today can be seen as typical of the period in which it was created. More striking, however, is its origin in the "spiritual type font" of Behrens, whose predilection for the triangle, the rectangle, and the square can still be seen particularly clearly in the three initial letters. Even in the unframed version there exists an architectonic system of order, for the three initials are consciously played off against each other in their differing forms yet held together by the power of their individual and mutual proportions.

The decorative borders for Behrens Roman and for the slightly earlier cursive version appeared in the AEG's newpapers as well as in its decorative addresses (illustrations G23-G29) and in literary collectors' items.7' The uncial version had been developed out of the cursive, and the two could be used together. Forerunners of these decorative patterns could be found in Behrens's interiors. Some had appeared in the designs for carpets and wall and floor inlays incorporating astragal, metope, and triglyph motifs,72 which had been developed in the Düsseldorf period (see illustration 8). The models for these were Tuscan incrustation motifs such as those at San Miniato in Florence. Similar motifs were also used on lamp holders, carved furnishings, and decorative latticework,73 and in AEG advertising material (illustrations G31, G52).

The decorative border surrounding the text of the Rathenau address ilustrations G16 and G24) suggests the splendid manuscripts of the court school of Charlemagne (for example the Godescalc Gospel Lectionary) and the St. Gereon Evangelium,74 with its many framing bands and rows of dotted decoration. The same dotted motif was also used by Behrens on the wall panels of the 1908 Schiffbau-Ausstellung pavilion (ilustration 5) and, in a reduced form, in some AEG publications (illustration G14). These dotted borders were either drawn up individually each time or assembled out of elements in the Behrens Roman font (illustration G44). The models for the wider borders with single decorative elements were ornamental manuscripts and—appropriately-—decorative ironwork, enamelwork,75 and filigree.

Behrens's ornamental technique and his method of interpreting his sources are demonstrated especially well by the capital D in illustrations G26, G27, and 13. In assembling the decorative collage, which is made up of Irish and pre-Carolingian elements, Behrens proceeded according to a principle that, although derived from the models themselves, is incomprehensible without an exact knowledge of Alois Riegl's book Die spätrömische Kunstindustrie nach Funden in Österreich-Ungarn.77 Riegl's account of the artistic methods of the "advanced Roman imperial age" had a great appeal to Behrens. These included the theories of motif and complementary motif and the techniques of achieving a coloristic effect in openwork and in chiseled bronzes with the help of "reciprocal patterns" and of the continuous rhythmic alternation of light and dark." Behrens developed a method of ordering his late-Roman-enriched repertoire of "contrasting curves," "angular forms," and "complementary positive and negative motifs"78 and of developing them in conjunction with kindred types of ornament. The initial D stands in a free relationship with the urrounding ornamentation. The letter itself is edged by a lighter contour which responds to the decorative pattern. The ornament never touches the lettering, but reacts to the architectural form of each initial as support, brace, or load, and sometimes responds with a dynamic flourish of its

![](images/86864435b8c75351f3441805ad955cdd7fd17bbcc9f0169f46c29f51478bbdbe.jpg)

Behrens decorative elements and late Roman chiseled bronze. From A. Riegl, Spätrömische Kunstindustrie (1927).

own. The ornament is made lighter and darker according to the "temperament" of the initial, which it frames in an almost imperceptible rectangle. The lighter, complementary ornament only follows the contours of light and gold-colored initials, ike the D in the Rathenau address, giving them a shimmering appearance. Otherwise, it abuts the white edging band of the dark letter without being developed out of it.

Behrens's treatment of the large tendril motif is an example of the way in which he gave a unified, "organic" form to one larger motif made up of a multiplicity of single elements. The small spiral tendril cantained within the D is taken out, enlarged, and unfurled down the page. In the course of this unwinding, all the smaller elements are hierachically ordered and molded to conform to the curl of the tendril. At no point is this principal form firmly delineated; rather, it is defined as the trace of a movement that starts out as a curve complementary to that of the letter D and pulls all the subsequent influences together intone formal statement. This large curve represents the portrayal of individual formal elements "by means of a rhythmic composition on the picture plane."o In contrast, the decorative pattern stamped into the leather cover of the Rathenau address (illustration G23) is much closer in spirit to industrial practice.

From the repertoire of late Roman decarative elements, Behrens chose exactly the one whose simplified form made it particularly suitable for endless repetition. In order ta create large forms in series, it is necessary to break the form dawn into the simplest elements, which can then be cambined in many different ways and reproduced endlessly. Such simple motifs were favored in the chisel technique by which bronze jewelry was embellished between the third and sixth centuries A.D. (illustration 16). The matifs Behrens derived from this model were based on the square and on a corner element that produces small "hearts" see illustration G. y developing the play f light and shadow in the riginal jewelry, Behrens arrived at four basic ornamental patterns. As a decorative principle, such a regular sequence of variations on one theme stands in total apposition t the arganic uniyof the letter and the domnant tendril moti. The late Roman practice according o Rg, was basednhe "eveling he basic form and the individual elements,"and led to "mass composition." The "isolation of the single element" that led to a "unifarm sequence of the same form," the "objectivity of appearance," the "resultant typical character," and the "anonymity that is always inseparably bound to such an anti-individualistic manner of artistic creation" (all mentioned by Rieg|8), ter with theinterdependence l themotion the picture plane are equally characteristic of Behrens's decorative style. Behrens—"an attentive reader Alois Riegl—utilized Rieg'concept Kunstolln, adapted the late-Roman principles of design, and translated Riegl's Kunstindustrie" into his own "Industriekunst."

All the Behrens scripts were manufactured by the Klingspor type fondry in Offenbach. Same were used intrial editions before being officially in troduced. The first publication set in Behrens Script, which was introduced officially in October 1902, was Festschrit der Künstlerkolonie: Ein Dokument Deutscher Kunst (1901). According to Rodenberg, the Behrens Cursive introduced in 1907-08 was arrived at by the circuitous route of cursive script. Behrens Raman first appeared in the AEG's guide ta its exhibition at the Schiffbau-Ausstellungof 1908.8 Behrens Medieval, afficially intraduced in 1914, underwent a trial printing in 1913. The decorative embellishments that went with Behrens Roman were put into production by the Klingspor campany and enjayed the same wide success as the typeface itself.

## The Advertising Graphics

The realm of art begins at the point where an object that has been simplifint n o beheial mol objects.   
Peter Behrens, 1900

The various phases in the develapment of Behrens's graphics can be seen as a historical sequence; the phases, however, aften averlapped. The geametric phase was characterized by the conspicuous system of framing that appeard, for example, in the Kunsthalle n Oldenburg illustration 18.According oBhrens,his intentn was solate he wall a make them independent by means of the framing," in order to demanevident in the poster for the 1906 Deutsche Jahrhundert-Ausstellung (ilustration 6). In the 1908 brachure for the AEG arc lamp factory (ilustration G40), the separation and enclosure of the triangular panel no longer serves to clarify the cantent but is a decorative end in itself. In 1906, Behrens develaped a decarative repertoire out of the architectural motifs he had employed in the crematorium at Hagen (illustration 15) and the Tonhaus der Flora in Cologne. This new repertoire embraced both the ornamental elements of the Behrens Roman font (for example, the "metope frieze" seen in ilustration 19) and abstract expressions of Behrens's design principles. The circle and the square in numerous combinations make up one of the basic themes.86 Although used mainly as a single motif, it was alsa multiplied in endless sequence in linaleum patterns, in the manner af Vasarely.

Peter Behrens, brochure for AEG metal-filament lamps, 1907. This and the following illustrations courtesy of Kunstbibliothek, Berlin.

This geometric formula was applied in the most memorable way by Behrens in his first graphic composition for the AEG, the cover designs for the 1906-1909 editions of Mitteilungen der Berliner Elektrizitätswerke (illustrations G1-G3). The 1908 cover is the most striking because of the way in which the central motif is accentuated; the complementary spandrel forms creote o spatial echo effect around a motif that to this day has lost nothing of its modernity.87 The first cover design of 190607 also achieves a spatial effect in spite of the rather precious scrolls; the dots in the corners of the frames increase in size and lead the eye progressively outward from the center. In Behrens's metal-filament-lamp poster of 1907 (illustration G30), probably his first poster design for the AEG, unifies the two main aspects of this phase in his work: the principle of framing, which analytically divides up the picture plane by oscribing more or less importance to the various parts, and the geometric system of ordering, which coordinates these frames into a pictorial image. The subject of the poster is thus isolated by the framework, and attention is focused toward it by the geometric structure. The symmetrical layout also embraces the dots that radiate out from the lamp with ever-decreasing intensity. A comparable poster by Eckmann (illustration 8 in Buddensieg's essay above) portrayed the same situation as an eruption of light. Although both posters have a certoin symbolic content, only the Behrens version, thanks to its "bosic mathematicol hormony," identified the "absolute precision of all the elements" in the picture with the product on display and with the firm that made it. Through the "unity of all the assembled parts"88 and the surrounding triangle, the intrinsic form of the light is highlighted.89 This is particularly clear in the negotive version of the poster which was included in the brochure for the lamp (illustration 22). The principle of Behrens's poster design, reduced to a formula, is that the geometry on the picture plane serves to accentuate the form of the object.

A similar system lay behind the composition of the title page of the 1908 catalog of Christmas tree lights shown in illustration G8. Here, however, the visible "guidelines" have disappeared, the lettering stands—rather exceptionally—in place of the surrounding arc, and the focus on the lights delineating the tree is intensified.

According to Gagel, "the most important stylistic prerequisite for the development of the descriptive poster [Sachplakat]—and also for the functional style [Sachstil]—was geometric purism." The gradual recession of this interpretative scheme in favor of a "rigorous orticulation of the planes"according to architectural principles and a "wonderfully harmonious distribution of the lettering" were to characterize the subsequent phases in the development of Behrens's graphic art. However, the "mathematical exactitude" ond the preference for symmetry remained, as the basis of the individual character of the AEG house style.91

What Gagel2 called the "tectonic or architectonic" style of advertising graphics in the pre-1914 period found its clearest expression in the work of Behrens. As the graphic vehicle for his architecturally derived aesthetic program he used the frame. Although allthe graphic designs of this pe-olle the same pncp  l  them portrayed the rame a symbolic architecture. The majority, however, did, and the frome was composed of pilasters, architraves, ond sills. The aim was to produce a standard format to which each new hand-drawn composition would conform. The format could then be reproduced at will from the existing printing blocks, and the drawings, product illustrations, lines and columns of print, numbers, specifications, and title banners could easily be related to each other (see illustrations G43, G44, G56, G57).

Behrens worked according to the theory of the interrelationship of concept ond scale; proportion was to him the force of order, and not only in the subdivision of the picture plone. He olso understood proportion in the figurative sense as the relationship between pictorial display and intended effect, between the object depicted and its intrinsic qualities. Even when, for reasons of pictorial content, Behrens abandoned the formal layout of pilaster and architrave, an invisible framework still remained to give a sense of order to the composition (see illustration G61).

In contrast to the two-tone, decorative splendor of the cover of the arclamp-factory brochure (illustration G40), the title page (illustration G41) carries a framed composition reminiscent of the facade of the AEG shop on the Potsdamer Strasse. The enclosed circle carrying the date and the blc o letteg inhe craveae par o theuilding,"wo proportions and formal, hierarchic composition are presented in a monumental manner. In this AEG proscenium orch, products or drawings could be displayed at will. The framework wos odjusted to suit the dimensions of the object on display, and additional pilosters or sils could be added to reduce the pictorial area (see illustrations G48, G49).

In the orc-lamp brochure, the lomps are reproduced in the most direct manner. This clarity set a pattern for a whole series of brochures. The layut, with a delicate frame surrounding the picture of the lomp (illustration G42), was cleorly meont for o small format. The high-quality color the ether. The surrounding space is indeterminately gray and shadowless a iustraton .  uratttineqs co, the beauty of the lamp casing is presented as the beauty of the simple object. Comparison with this makes it clear that in the 1909 silhouette presentation of the arc lamp (ilustration G43) the message to be conveyed was the unique ond unmistakable form of the lamp. Here the form was abstracted from the object as a combination of elegant two-dimensional elements derived from the simple outlines of sphere, cone, and cylinder. The form of the arc lamp was thus given the character of a framework.

Behrens created another type of abstract image in his design for the quartz-lamp brochure of 1908 (ilustration G39. The lamp was portrayed neither in its spatial actuality nor in its pure two-dimensional form. Instead, two curving lines were offered by Behrens to symbolize the radiating light. The enclosure of this symbolic form within a rigidly symmetrical frame, with a capricious Roman script, freed the double curve of any concrete designation.

The framed presentations described above are found almost exclusively in small-format works. An exception, however, is the metal-filament-lamp "icon" shown in illustration G30 (clearly a later poster that should be considered with the next group). The arched frame, decorated with the splendid ornament of the Behrens Roman script, intensifies the impact of the isolated, reefloatgu.

The more pictorial manner of poster design, which was also adopted for small-format publicity material such as leaflets and stamps (illustration G74), falls into two phases. Common to both is the copying of photographs of the product; this graphic presentation, however, differed in the two phases.

The first phase can be characterized as the painterly phase. In illustration G55 (cf. illustration P37), the electric fan is shown in three-quarter view— an angle Behrens otherwise avoided, as it went against his belief that static and symmetrical views were the most striking. The schematicized effects on the fan of light and shadow are analyzed in delicate shades of gray. Although this does not correspond to the original color scheme, it gave the picture of the fan an artistic quality init own right. It also gave the poster, with its midnight-blue background, a greater impact than any photograph could have achieved, especially from a distance. The isolation of, say, the image of a light bulb in front of a strongly colored but completely flat background recalls the techniques of commercial photography.

This alienation heightened the plasticity of the lamp image, which when jutaposed with the firm's initials and the tile achieved an almost iconic quality. This tendency was developed further in the second type of presentation.

In the second technique of reinterpreting photographic images there are no longer any illusionistic shadows. The poster and prospectus for the metal-filament lamp (illustrations G32-G37) show how the graphic quality of the shadow was transformed into strongly defined dark areas set in a complementary relationship with the lighter half of the screw fitting. The contour of the bulb is taken directly from the real bulb and yet emancipates itself as an autonomous graphic image. This image, freed from reference to any external factors, is responsible only to the proportional system established by the frame and the lettering. The graphic form itself is given the character of a signet. The 1907 metal-filament-lamp poster shown in illustration G30 creates spatial references through the framework of lines, but quite different are the lamp posters shown in illustrations G32-G37. Compositionally, these aim inward toward the subject of the poster, and according to Behrens they were not conceived as "an architectural detail, through which a spatial idea could be realized." Characteristic of this type of poster is the way in which the object "stands out from the uniformity of its surroundings." "Herein," said Behrens, "lies not only its commercial success; but also its artistic originality."4

The technique of focusing on the simply drawn light bulb, without any reference to its functional context, also appeared at this time in the work of other graphic artists who were active in Berlin.

23   
Lucian Bernhard, poster for Adler typewriters, 1907.

This "Schlagschattengrafik" [heavy-shadow graphics] technique, which was used principally for depicting technical objects and machines, had two roots: the traditional woodcut and the retouched photograph which had taken its place in technical publications. In order to clarify the general relationships between the parts, both techniques used an intensified contrast of light and dark. Illustration G63 shows a combination of both techniques.

In the process of transformation from object to photograph to graphic image, only the full tones and hard contrasts were retained in the graphic material showing the metal-filament lamp. To maximize the effect of the poster at a distance, Behrens simplified the forms and eliminated all the half-tones. Behrens's contemporary Lucian Bernhard had already used this method in 1907-08 in his poster for Adler typewriters (ilustration 23), and in doing so had established his own poster style. Particularly in his industrial graphics, Bernhard was to perfect a presentation based on pure contrasts. This technique was also to appear in some of the AEG publicity material. Leaflets with interpretations of Behrens's framing system in the style of Bernhard,6 as well as many brochures showing factory silhouettes,7 support the supposition the Bernhard worked with Behrens.98 (On Behrens's advice, signatures were dispensed with in brochures and posters.) Among the AEG designs in this style were the publicity seals shown in illustration G74 and the graphics in the 1908 steam-turbine catalog (illustrations 19 and G59). However, the dominating diagonal that was so typical of Bernhard's compositions never gained the upper hand over Behrens's principles of axiality and symmetry. Bernhard's dynamic method of composition and the easy appeal of his graphics stood in direct opposition to Behrens's architectonic and symbolic conception of design. There were, nevertheless, technical similarities, such as the double border around the trademark.

## Einfachheit ist Relchtum, Sis Berlian.

Nach vielem Gribeln und nach jabrelangem Studium habe ich endich den Weg gefunden, den ich seit der Zeit uce.s ich n noch nct att. .Renaissance' ist ja schlieflich auch kein unbler Stil, Materiat stand damals den Ausfhrenden in genogender Menge tur Verfugung, aber heutzutago, wn alles nach einer einfachen, schlichten Form schreit, wo man sich in einem Zimmer wohl fahlt, in dem wohi fehNt, was ein Ater Empfindender unter keinen Umstünden vermissen möchle, sind wir doch dem stilisierten Lukas mit der Glahnase, t n en Gesichtam p. schuld ahh mode-kunsterisch zu wirken.

![](images/10390565a49d8f339f1fc9fe718ce6e027918773359aafabebb38db8c18bb7c5.jpg)

![](images/0204296f9dc9c67be61d13316bbd0a320fa0a57c3beffe66ddc0ce1be725cbf5.jpg)

Throughout the period of Behrens's activity for the AEG, other graphic designers and agencies also produced advertising material for the company, occasionally supplying rather old-fashioned reinterpretations of Behrens's models (see, for example, illustration G709). For many of the other designers, however, Behrens's layouts, script forms, and graphic techniques provided the framework within which they could realize their own ideas in a more or less uniform manner. Furthermore, so much everyday and humdrum printed material was produced to Behrens's design that the penetration of his graphic manner could be felt everywhere, and was influential even outside the AEG. Yet toward the end of Behrens's activity in the firm a new trend gained the ascendancy over his variable system of standard layouts and led to an uncontrolled multiplicity of styles such as had been usual before 1907. The appearance of the artist's signature on brochures marked the end of the anonymous company style. In 1916, Behrens was on the jury that selected designs for postcards portraying the nitrogen lamp ilustration G86). These designs make clear the move toward the figurative poster.

Behrens's unique ability to find symbols for technical appliances and their functions, and to set them in a suggestive balance against the accompanying text, may also be illustrated by one more striking example. Just as the quartz-lamp brochure took a particular place in the group of framed designs, so the searchlight brochures of 1909 and 1911 (illustrations G50, G51) were exceptional examples within the second category of illustrated designs. Not only do they have the usual three elements of illust, lettea barun u eseahlight e reuc signet.

Characteristic of all the different types of advertising graphics produced by Behrens is the abstraction and reduction of both the diverse and the systematic down to a typical symbol. Form here was that quality which was propagated by the spirit of the modern age. Form stood above—or, better, embraced—the material around which it was pounded into shape. But, according to both Behrens and Riegl, form is dominant over material. As the physical embodiment of an idea, form is independent of material, although material considerations can give substance and direction to the idea. Behrens's graphic works illustrated this physical embodiment of an idea. The process of abstracting an unmistakable type out of the unique qualities of a particular form corresponded to the stated aim of creating types that would be not only universally valid, but also generally understandable.

With so much abstraction and so little room for individuality, the artists in Behrens's studio might have found working for the AEG tedious and stultifying. Yet writing the same titles to order again and again, never being allowed a private flourish, constantly having to repeat the same layout, and loading every page with the message of Behrens's "beautiful new form" and the weight of the firm's pretensions did not lead to the standardization of their brains. This is shown by the AEG's Bierzeitung (beer newspaper). (The Bierzeitung, a comic or satirical magazine, had its origin in the German student custom of the Kneipe.) Besides publishing the first example of the brochure design that would be used for the arc lamps (illustration G40) and the first evidence of the collaboration of louis Oppenheim (illustration G58), the AEG-Bierzeitung also rolled a beer barrel into Behrens's sacred framework and spoofed Behrens's factory windows lillustration 29). That the critical fantasy of the Bierzeitung's contributors was contained within Behrensian proportions and did not spill over like foaming beer can probably be ascribed to the master's omnipresent influence.

For the Kunsthalle in Oldenburg (1905) Behrens develaped a formal purism based on a simple pattern of squares, diagonals, and triangles. This pattern was used on allthe buildings and in the garden layout, and provided the basis for the technique of abstractian that crystallized in the Berlin pavilion. Reduced to the most basic forms, the outline and image of the Berlin pavilion are as clear as a drawing. In contrast, the garden layout uses different basic farms: the square, the sphere, and the pyramid. Similar design techniques are to be found in Behrens's ornamental graphics.

# Peter Behrens and the AEG's Railway Projects

Sabine Bohle

During his employment with the AEG, Peter Behrens had several opportunities to work on the problems of rapid-transit railway systems. He designed a railway viaduct and a station, and also the internal layout and fittings of railway carriages—then, as now, an unusual job for an architect. The railway designs Behrens created in connection with the AEG's rapid-transit project of 1907 count without doubt among the most notable of his works. Because of the many time-consuming difficulties encountered in the planning phase, the project was never implemented; as a result, it has been given little attention until now.

In 1907 the AEG presented a plan for a railway from Gesundbrunnen to Neukölln. This plan was based on preparatory studies going back to 18,and was an alternative o the suspended-railway projec he Continentale Gesellschaft fur elektrische Unternehmungen, based in Nuremberg.² Although the proposed routes were almost identical, the AEG's design had certain advantages trom the outset over the suspended railway. One disadvantage of the latter was its unusual basic principle, which made it extremely difficult to integrate into the existing railway network. An even more serious factor was the general antipathy of the authorities toward any form of overhead railway.³ The AEG proposal also failed to gain the immediate approval of the authorities, for, with the exception of a2.4-kilometer underground section between Invalidenstrasse and Spree, it too called for an overhead railway. Before a contract could be made with the city of Berlin, the AEG was forced to make extensive changes to its program.

New plans presented in July 1909 proposed that the track be taken underground from Humboldthain to Urbanhafen. Economic considerations mitigated against putting the entire line underground. As the city insisted that the track be underground as far as Hermannplatz, it offered after lengthy negotiations to carry some of the additional costs that would result. The contract between the city of Berlin and the AEG was signed on 18 March 1912, and with that the idea of a suspended railway was fi-1914.

According to the cantract, only the end section af the line to the north of Grenzallee was to be constructed as an averhead railway. This section took in the three stations: Prinzenallee, Christianastrasse, and Gesundbrunnen. Constructian work began on the Brunnenstrasse in the spring of 1914. When it was halted in October 1919,4 no start whatsoever had been made on the overhead section. After the Nordsüdbahn AG took up the work again in 1926, the development of the overhead section was deferred and later completely abandoned.

As a result of these changes of plan, many of the designs for the railway—particularly those for the elevated sectian—were never executed. Notable among these were Behrens's 1910 designs for a high-level viaduct and a statian. Anather unexecuted Behrens scheme was that far the Neanderstrasse underground station (ilustration 1).

The model of the underground statian differed anly in detail from the normal Berlin concept, but the designs for the averhead railway reveal a much more adventurous approach. The idea of supporting a viaduct on single, central pilars (illustration A177) went back to Werner von Siemens's 1880 plan far an overhead railway an the Friedrichstrasse. An earlier madel far this type af railway was alsa affered by the "elevates" in New York. Behrens consciously chose the single support system, as it taok up less raam than the partal support system of Siemens and Halske and was therefore more suitable for the proposed AEG line, most af which was ta run along narrow streets.

In designing the viaduct, Behrens adhered ta a strictly functional viewpoint. As in the Siemens system, the supporting pillars were to be riveted together out of simple steel girders; however, they were to be more robust than the Siemens version. On the upper level, Behrens planned to use salid, riveted panels, which, although lacking the lightness and transparency af the custamary latticewark railings, were simple to make and less prone to corrosion. The rails were to rest on widely projecting, clamp-shaped brackets reaching up to the top of the side panels. Like the whole conception, these unadorned brackets were a functionally appropriate response to a series of complex technical demands.

![](images/f78486d1ef3698a8cf2c049bf3c8e358adba0a4c3937b86fa6df9de262c88819.jpg)  
Model of Neanderstrasse subway station. From MdBEW.

The two versions af the design for a high-level station reveal Behrens's fundamentally new conception of form even better. Although the design cannot be related to any particular station, the unusually large dimensions suggest that this was not a design for a normal through-station, but was for the terminus at Gesundbrunnen (the station nearest the AEG works at Humboldthain).

Behrens farmulated his new principles of station design more clearly in the line drawing published by Paul Wittig in 19227 (illustration A176) than in his preliminary study.8 He abandoned the arched or saddleback raaf forms customarily used on sheds of this type. Instead, he chose to use a flat roof, which further emphasized the cubiform impression made by the unusually long complex. A particular accent was given by the projecting transverse pavilion separating the enclased stairwell from the platforms. The forms of this pavilion display a certain Classicism. A similar tendency appeared in the flat, stepped roof, which made a clear reference to the St. Petersburg embassy. The same is true of the entrance, with its strongly accentuated pilasters.

In contrast, the broad, bandlike window Behrens wrapped araund the building pointed in a completely new direction. This matif was ta be thoroughly developed by other architects, and in particular by Behrens's pupils Mies and Gropius. Not very much of this is to be found in the preliminary study to this design ilustration A175), in which Behrens differentiated the various parts of the building much mare strongly. The long facade was subdivided by several transverse pavilions, and the building was topped by an entablature which became an attic stary an the pavilions. In addition, the roaf covering the stairwell was extended ta give mare weight to the end facade, and this facade was alsa emphasized by the powerful detailing of the corners. This led to a rather unsettled general impressian, which in a certain sense contradicts the boldness and resolution of the line drawing. The unfinished, almast sketchy character of the preliminary study also shaws in the small drawings on the left-hand side of the sheet abave and below the viaduct, which might be taken s altenative suggestions for the entrance and the windaws.

After this intensive activity on the AEG's overhead railway project, which sadly never went beyond the planning stage, it was only a short step for Behrens ta the designing af railway cars.

In 1914-15 the AEG developed a 90-horsepower diesel-electric locomoi ohenorial  itcar were built by the Steinfurt campany in Känigsberg to Behrens's design, and were unveiled in the AEG-Mitteilungen in 1917."° Behrens's collaboratian was referred to in the follawing shart nate: "The carriagewark was supplied by the L. Steinfurt railway works, and this firm was advised by the well-known Berlin architect Professor Peter Behrens."

The locomotive was an experimental madel which, on the basis of its form, can be seen as the forerunner of standard branch-line driving units. Little is known about its life as a locomotive. In 1935 it was sold without its matar by the railway scrap merchant "Erich am Ende" in Berlin-Weissensee ta the Neukälln-Mittenwald railway, which used it as a "Beiwagen" until 1967, when it was finally scrapped. The AEG locomotive introduced several technical innovatians, mast natably the combined diesel-electric pawer and the relacatian af the pawer unit to the interior. Even more striking, however, were the innavatians in the design of the carriagework and the campartments. The exterior of Behrens's rail cars was distinguished by braad windows with rounded upper corners. The windows could be lawered, and together with the air vents above pravided the necessary ventilatian. Additional ventilation ducts (later remaved) were built into the raaf at the frant of the unit to extract the extra heat generated in the engine roam. As there was a driving position at either end, the back and the frant were given essentially the same form. Four equally sized windows extended acrass the entire width. The outside windows were set at a slightly ablique angle (by no means a normal practice at that time) for the sake of visibility.

![](images/791ff9e1dbf934c2851a661fa25e8e13337904438772f628975f5e440188a220.jpg)  
AEG diesel-electric rail car, 1914-15. Cremers 1928, p. 76.  
AEG diesel-electric rail car photographed in 1964. Courtesy of Sigurd Hilkenbach.

Inoddition to the two drivig positions nd the engine room, the ineor of the or wos divied into three thir-closs coportents otalng tty seats ond one second-closs comportment with ten seots. The two classes were divided by o portition with a door in it. The second-closs section wos entered vio the reor driving position, ond the third-closs section via 0 gongwoy lcoted between he reor drivig positionnd the engie room.

The interior loyout ond fittings, described by Georg Königshogen" as "eigenartig" (singular; originol), clearly reveoled Behrens's personol style ond his preference for cleor, lucid forms. Königshogen confirmed thot the interior design wos corried out according to the instructions of Behrens.'2 Very striking wos the subdivision of the third-closs comportments by ponels, with strongly molded frames ond inclined edges, which ron to holf the height of the car ond looked olmost like extended seot bocks. This daring method of dividing the comportments onticipoted todoy's express cars and made o morked controst to the then-customory method of seporoting the compartments by roof-high portitions. In the obsence of such portitions, o new place had to be found for the luggoge rocks. Behrens set them along the side wolls above the windows, thus ochieving an even stronger impression of spaciousness ond clority in the interior.

Dark-stained ook wos chosen for the woll poneling in the third-closs section and in the entronce gongway. The seots were pointed block ond hod simple wooden bockrests. The floor wos covered in green linoleum, ond the roof was painted white.

In the secand-class sectian, camfartable seats cavered in striped plush were arranged araund the walls facing a swiveling aval table. As was custamary i secan-class cartents, the wall were cad inany. Behrens's railway designs are anly ane stane in the extensive masaic af his wark. In their clear and precise farmal language they reveal a sure understanding af technical and canstructianal cansideratians.
