## Invitation Card to Exhibition of Tools and Appliances 1910

This invitation was sent out by the AEG to publicize its newly opened exhibition of electrical tools and household appliances in the shop at 4 Königgratzer Strasse. The text, in Behrens Roman script, was printed in black on an ivory-colored background. The initial D was decorated in gold (cf. G24), and the border of double volutes in gold was framed by a black line. The size of the frame was 22.1 x 15.3 cm.

## West End Salesroom, 117 Potsdamer Strasse, Berlin 1910

The door and window surrounds of the salesroom were white marble panels inside dark frames. The window shelves were arranged according to the products on display, with a three-sided backing screen (sometimes with a poster in the central panel) (see JDW 1913, illustration 93). The door had a honeycomb AEG trademark on glass (see G75 ff.), and the square fanlight contained a circular extractor fan set in a geometric framework. The interior walls and ceiling were geometrically articulated, with wood and stucco coffering and moldings.

## References

Stoedtner photos 71976 (shopfront), 72522 (interior); Mannheimer 1911, illustration p. 137; JDW 1912, illustration 64; Hoeber 1913, pp. 155 ff. and illustrations 187 and 188; JDW 1913, illustration 93; Meyer-Schönbrunn 1913, illustrations 49 and 51; Deutsche Bauhütte 18 (1914), llustrations pp. 110, 114, and 127; MdBEW 11, no. 11 (November 1915), p. 176.

The basement af the administration building, originally planned by Alfred Messel as a canteen (see plans, TU Berlin, inventary nas. 12057 and 12067), was canverted in 1908 ta house a permanent exhibition af AEG products. Behrens was prabably involved in the canversian.

## P16 Arc Lamp 1907

Differential AC arc lamp designed to produce shadowless, nondazzling, indirect illumination for interiors. The light was thrown up onto the ceiling by the larger reflector and from there shone down diffusely into the room.

## Types

Two different types with varying burning times of approximately 8-10 and 13-16 hours were offered: length 820 mm, reflector diameter 750 mm, 68 marks (1909), PL no. 68216; length 1,000 mm, reflector diameter 750 mm, 74 marks (1909), PL no. 68218.

## Finish

Reflecor: sheet metal, painted light green with bronze trim.

## References

AEG-Bogenlampliste, no. 7 (1909), pp. 10-11; Stoedtner photo 49383; Scheffler 1908, p. 433; Monasch 1909, p. 14; AEG-Ztg. 11, no. 12 (June 1909), p. 6; MdBEW 7, no. 7 (July 1911); Pudor 1911, p. 297; Hoeber 1913, p. 106, illustration 111; Casabella 160, p. 24 (lamp illustrated upside down); T.M. AEG-Telefunken 60, no. 3 (1970), p. 194; Buddensieg and Rogge 1977, p. 127, illustration 7, p. 138 (with earlier model).

![](images/205fe67b548d89e8457e3dc308a7a9c7e4ccc1319e9f0c5f491f373e404cb784.jpg)

## P17 Arc Lamp 1907

Differential DC arc lamp with inverted rods (positive rod below) for interior lighting. The small reflector bounced light off the ceiling.

## Types

Two different types were offered, with burning times of approximately 10 and 16 hours: length 820 mm, reflector diameter 600 mm, 64 marks (1909), PL no. 68220; length 1,000 mm, reflector diameter 600 mm, 70 marks (1909), PL no. 68222.

## Finish

Lamp painted light green with oz gla .

## References

AEG-Bogenlampliste, no. 7 (1909), pp. 8-9; Stoedtner photo 49383; Scheffler 1908, p. 433; Monasch 1909, p. 14; Pudor 1911, p. 297; Buddensieg and Rogge 1977, p. 128, illustration 11, p. 138 (with earlier model).

![](images/298b10584e3408e09cdaeb8cf65462e112d08419702f12d1cd78f0b6efad3ee9.jpg)

## P18 Arc Lamp 1907

Differential DC arc lamp for interior lighting, particularly intended for precision work such as that dane in a print shop. Semi-indirect illumination was achieved thraugh reflecting the light off the ceiling and also through the direct but diffuse light shining through the opal-glass reflector.

## Types

Two different types were offered, with burning times af approximately 10 and 16 hours: length 820 mm, reflector diameter 600 mm, 64 marks (1909), PL no. 68220; length 1,000 mm, reflector diameter 1,000 mm, 70 marks (1909), PL no. 68222.

## Finish

Lamp painted light green with bronze trim, apal-glass reflector.

## References

AEG-Bogenlampliste, no. 7 (1909), pp. 8-9; Stoedtner photo 49383; Scheffler 1908, p. 433; Monasch 0 p. 14; Pudor 1911, p. 297; Buddensieg and Rogge 1977, p. 128, illustration 11, p. 138 (with earlier model).

## P19 Arc Lamp 1907

Differential DC arc lamp with inverted rads (as P17) far interiar lighting. Particularly suited for workshops. Semi-indirect illumination was achieved by the same means as in P18.

## Types

Two types were offered, with burning times of appraximately 10 and 16 haurs: length 820 mm, glass globe diameter 320 mm, 51 marks (1909), PL no. 68208; length 1,000 mm, glass glabe diameter 320 mm, 57 marks (1909), PL no. 68210.

## Finish

Lamp painted light green with bronze trim, matte-glass glabe.

## References

AEG Bogenlampenliste, no. 7 (1909), pp. 4-5; Hagen photo 13124; Scheffler 1908, p. 433; Widmer 1908, p. 50; Pudar 1911, p. 297; Hellwag 1911, p. 152 (with earlier madel; JDW 191 2, p. 67; Buddensieg and Rogge 1977, p.12illustration 1p. 138139.

## P20 Arc Lamp 1907

Arc lamp (AC or DC) for indirect lighting with inverted rads las P17), small reflector, and glass cover. Lamps with exposed arcs could not be used in raams containing easily inflammable materials, for example in spinning or weaving mills. It was necessary, therefore, ta enclose the entire lamp.

## Types

Two types were offered, with burning times af appraximately 10 and 16 haurs: length 820 mm, reflectar diameter 350 mm, 68 marks (1909), PL no. 68228; length 1,000 mm, reflector diameter 350 mm, 74 marks (1909), PL na. 68230.

## Finish

Sheet-metal reflectar painted light green with bronze trim, clear glass shield between armature and reflector.

## References

AEG-Bogenlampenliste, na. 7 (1909), pp. 1415; Pudor 1911, p. 297; Buddensieg and Rogge ia 139.

## P21 Arc Lamp 1907

Arc lomp (AC or DC) for semi-indirect lighting with inverted rods ond sphericol gloss globe (see G37). The motte-glass lower holf of the globe reflected most of the light upword. The some model wos ovoiloble with normol rods. Both types were intended, like P20, for use in flommoble surroundings. The burning time was around 10 hours. Length 820 mm, globe diometer 430 mm, 68 marks (1909), PL no. 68212.

## Finish

Pointed light green with bronze trim and cap. Gloss globe: upper holf cleor, lower half matte.

## References

AEG-Bogenlampenliste, no. 7 (0), pp. 13-14; Buddensieg - tion 12b, p. 139.

## P22, P23 Arc Lamp 1907

Arc lamp (AC or DC) with inverted rods for semi-indirect lighting, with small motte-gloss shode (os P19) ond undulating diffuser (P22). In rooms thot were too dork, too high, or not pointed white, the reflection of the light hod to be oided by o platelike reflector mounted on the upper port of the lamp. The plote wos either flot or given concentric undulations to create o porticulorly effective diffuse light. In oddition to this model, the following lamps were olso ovailoble with upper reflectors or diffusers: PL no. 68200 (see P17), PL no. 68212 (see P21), PL no. 68228 (see P20), ond PL no. 68212 for olternoting current (P23).

## Types

Two types were offered, with burning time of approx. 10 ond 16 hours: length 820 mm, globe diometer 320 mm, 72.50 morks (1909), PL no. 68208 D; length 1,000 mm, globe diameter 320 mm, 78.50 morks (1909), Pl no. 68210 D. Diffuser diameter 1,000 mm, upper reflector diometer 860 mm.

## Finish

As P19; diffuser white enomel.

## References

AEG Bogenlampenliste, no. 7 (1909), pp. 1617; Stoedtner photo 49388; Monasch 1909, p. 14; Pudor 1911, p. 297; Monnheimer 1911, p. 140; Pevsner 1957, p. 115 ond illustrotion 82 (wrongly described os o street light); Buddensieg ond Rogge 7  1ilusttion  . 139 (with eorlier model).

## Economy Arc Lamp 1907

Arc lamp with downward-facing reflector for direct lighting in rooms with low ceilings. Like that of the long-life arc lamp, the air intake of the economy lamp was smaller than that of an open lamp, so that the carbon rads burned more slowly. But the economy lamp was brighter than the long-le lamp, it used thinner rods.

## Types

Two different types were affered: length ca. 675 mm, diameter max. 350 mm (the so-called large economy lamp, model a); Length ca. 530 mm, diameter max. 250 mm (the so-called small economy lamp, model b). The lamp was also supplied with an automatic resistor mounted above the cap (model c) and with a matte-glass globe for semi-indirect lighting model  ee P25, P26).

## Finish

Painted dark green, with gold rims and rivets.

## References

## High-Intensity Arc Lamp 1908

High-intensity arc lamp with reflectar and glass glabe far direct lighting aut af doors (model a). In cantrast ta the narmal, vertical alignment, the rads in this lamp were maunted diaganally with the paints dawn. The increased illuminatian achieved by this arrangement cauld be further impraved by adding luminaus salts ta the rads. The resulting steam made the lamp suitable anly far very large raams ar for external use. Model b, withaut a reflectar and with a prismatic, flashed glass globe, was affered far indaar use (see P28). Length ca. 825 mm, diameter max. 240 mm.

Painted dark green, with gald rims.

## Triplex Arc Lamp 1908

Triplex arc lamp for direct illumination through a cylindrical, flashed opal-glass globe (madel a). As in P27 and P28, luminous salts were added to the vertically aligned carban rods, so that the lamp could only be used outdoors or in large internal spaces. This lamp was also available with a spherical opalglass globe (model b).

## Types

Length ca. 925 or 1,076 mm, diameter max. 270 mm (model a); length ca. 1,040 mm, diameter max. 350 mm (madel b).

Finish As P27.

## References

Hagen photo 13780; Stoedtner photo 49365 (model b); AEG-Bogenlampenfabrik, catalag (1908); MdBEW 4, no. 8 (August 1908), p. 116; AEG-Ztg. 11, no. 12 (June 1909), p. 6, figure 2; Int. P30 Arc Lamp ca. 1908-09

This differential arc lamp for direct lighting had a simple mechanism, burned almast silently, and could be cannected singly or in series.

## Types

## Flammeco Lamp 1910

Flammeco lamp with flat reflector for direct external lighting in sations, factory halls, and other places where continuaus illuminatian was required. This was a longburning model, and was available for factory halls with a deeper reflectar (model a; see G45).

## Types

Two types were affered: length ca. 985 mm, diameter 400 mm (for alternating current); length ca. 840 mm, diameter 400 mm (far direct current).

Finish A P27.

![](images/6406d40055d91461725024fe94b5ff684d0f772e8f334ffa8319206703dd2968.jpg)

## P32

## Nitralamp 1913

Nitralamp or half-watt lamp without reflector for direct external and internal lighting. This technically superior incandescent lamp replaced the arc lamp in many places, particularly in street lighting, shop-window illumination, and floodlighting. It provided powerful illumination at low cost. The tungsten wire filament was encased in a nitrogen-filled globe. The lamp was also offered with a reflector (see P33 and G47).

## Types

Two different types were offered: length 540 mm, diameter max. 270 mm; length 650 mm, diameter max. 345 mm.

## Finish

Painted black, with an opal-glass globe.

## References

Int. AEG-Ztg. 16, no. 4 (October 1913), suppls.; MdBEW, 9, no. 10 (October 1913), pp. 152 ff.; ibid. 9, no. 11 (November 1913), pp. 167169; ibid. 10, no. 3 (March 1914), p. 36 (special armature); ibid. 10, no. 11 (November 1914), p. 160; Buddensieg and Rogge 1977, p. 135, illustrations 32 and 33, p. 142.

## P33

## Nitralamp with Reflector 1913

See P32 for description and references. Two types were offered: length 670 mm, maximum diameter 450 mm; length 670 mm, maximum diameter 550 mm.

![](images/e5a97102833fe077c470605fd77af913b82ec29c7ac6aa0e9c3f01fc73b71611.jpg)  
Die Halbwatt-Lampe der AE G mit AuBenreflektor

## P34 Pulley Assemblies for Arc Lamps ca. 190809

Two models of pulley assemblies were offered, one for all types of arc lamps and the other for the "small economy arc lamp." PL no. 68925, with cable lengths of 13, 19, 25, and 31 m, sold for 72 marks in 1909; Pl no. 68921, with cable lengths of 9, 10, 11.5, and 13.5 m, sold for 32 marks. The construction was brass, painted dark green.

## References

Int. AEG-Ztg. 12, no. 4 (October 109, suppl. 6; Buddensieg and Rogge 1977, p. 131, illustration 22.

![](images/71b92fd4911d6b4fc158ac675053bf021265eafa980090082705a4ac23063026.jpg)

![](images/4bdd144e99f51b18422da059ecb54d2869f8bc12ec64a0852939cbeda724dc93.jpg)  
P34 From Interne AEG-Zeitung.

## P35 High-Intensity Arc Lamp, Installed 1908

This photograph (AEG photo no. 7279, 17 September 1910) shows the high-intensity arc lamp for external illumination (P27) mounted outside the AEG high-voltage factory at the Brunnenstrasse site in Berlin-Wedding (see A54-A73). The supporting brackets were also designed by Behrens.

![](images/c17b9e6eb2df97c24c0b69411d6de4d0fdebbed87f0d7e4b398f0fb1a1b1a672.jpg)

P36

# Lighting Scheme for Ceiling of Keller & Reiner Gallery, Berlin 1910

Incondescent bulbs and crystal glass prisms were strung together in chains here. The miniature lamps were originally used only for decorative lighting, and this scheme was intended to prove thot they could olso be used for continuous illuminotion. The diffuse lighting, stemming from many separate points, wos further reflected ond refrocted by the prisms. By freeing the lamps from any constructionol framework, Behrens enhanced the impression of insubstontiolity and the ornamental choracter of the scheme.

## References

P36

Stoedtner photos. 71973, 72521; MDBEW 6, no. 3 (March 1910), pp. 40-43; AEG-Ztg. 12, no. 9 (March 1910), p. 7; Michel 1910, pp. 130131; Dettmor 1911, pp. 1718, illustrotion 11; Hoeber 1913, pp. 130131, 142143.

Cover Design for Fan

![](images/c848adc7100e58ad9ca8882b8adb41069b184f6b95448cd64ccb89d85642ad80.jpg)

P38, P39 Table Fan 1908

Variations of this model were available on a pivoting stand (see P45), with a wall mounting (P48), and as an ozone ventilator (P39). All could be supplied for direct, alternating, or three-phase current. Four different motors were offered.

GBO: 15-watt motor, height 235 mm, blade diameter 200 mm, guard diameter 220 mm, 1912 price 23.50 marks, Pl no. 66001 GB1: 25-watt motor, height 290 mm, blade diameter 250 mm, guard diameter 270 mm, 1912 price 31 marks, Pl no. 66011 GB2: 45-watt motor, height 360 mm, blade diameter 300 mm, guard diameter 340 mm, 1912 price 33.50 marks, Pl no. 66021 GB3: 70-watt motor, height 420 mm, blade diameter 360 mm, guard diameter 400 mm, 1912 price 44 marks, PL no. 66031.

## Finish

The cast-iron motor housing was painted green, with tombac bands, and the blades were of polished brass.

P41

## Table Fan, Earlier Model

This fan, which could be supplied on a fixed base or with a wall fitting, had nickel-plated blades and a black-lacquered motor casing with gold lines. More elegant models were nickel-plated or galvanized in a bronze color.

![](images/db35eb4490330741a66ded894b4ae3101c4de5a60783a546ead1c4accf41e84c.jpg)  
P41

## P42, P43 Table Fan 1908

This model differed from P38 because it was designed specifically for alternating current. The speed could not be regulated, and the motor had a flatter profile. The simple forms of the casing and the base were retained. Four different motors were offered.

WBO: 25-watt motor, height 235 mm, blade diameter 200 mm, guard diameter 220 mm, 1912 price 23.50 marks, Pl no. 66301 WB1: 35-watt motor, height 290 mm, blade diameter 290 mm, guard diameter 270 mm, 1912 price 31 marks, Pl no. 66311 WB2: 70-watt motor, height 415 mm, blade diameter 300 mm, guard diameter 340 mm, 1912 price 52 marks, Pl no. 66321 WB3: 85-watt motor, height 445 mm, blade diameter 360 mm, guard diameter 400 mm, 1912 price 78 marks, Pl no. 66331. The finish was the same as that of P38.

## References

AEG photo 14445; Hagen photos (unnumbered); AEG photo 14178 (preliminary drawing); Int. AEG-Ztg. 14, no. 11 (May 1912), suppl. 8 (price list and preliminary drawing).

## P45 Table Fan ca. 1908

This pivot-mounted version of P38 is decorated with a cube with three stepped planes on its four outer faces, which acts as a visual connection between the base and the forks holding the motor.

Reference AEG photo 14463.

![](images/d724dbbf3d5d1b83f05fbab7585ca68d5707dc18f3bde59a53c7240dbfcb4b75.jpg)

## P46

## Table Fan with Stylized Guard ca. 1908

The stylized struts of the guard echoed the turning motion of the fan. Models with this type of guard were also made for wall mounting. The cast-iron motor housing was painted green, with tombac bands, and the blades were of polished brass.

## References

Hagen photo (no number); Scheffler 1908, p. 434 (wall-mounted model; Widmer 1909, p. 51 (wallmounted model).

![](images/8692873ce4e74541bcd0e96158e0603fe32546c86e8e60539cf971acba0e8090.jpg)  
P46 Courtesy f Karl Ernst Ostaus Museum, Hagen.

P47

## Wall-Mounted Fan with Flexible Motor Mounting ca. 1908

The special feature of this generalpurpose DC fan was the flexible mounting of the motor, which could be swiveled through 90 degrees on its bracket and tilted through 60 degrees on its pivots. To the molded cube was added a tombac-plated ferrule, which, as both decorative element and connector, linked the motor and the bracket. The cast-iron motor housing was painted green, with tombac bands, and the blades were of polished brass.

## References

AEG photos 14377, 13966, 14464; Hellwag 1911, p. 155 (with ilustration of an earlier modell).

![](images/393c26b49c68e94372f83b1fdad60b3ddb13d09d2c8c91d72b1e144e24ae8656.jpg)  
P47

P48

## Wall-Mounted Fan ca. 1908

This AC fan was wall-mounted on a fixed bracket. In contrast with P47, the cube on this model has molding with a circle superimposed on a square, and a cylindrical tombac ferrule. The cast-iron motor housing was painted green, with tombac bands, and the blades were of polished brass.

## References

Hagen photo 12520, unnumbered Hagen photo (front view); JDW 1912, p. 66; Pevsner 1968, pp. 172173 (wrongly identified as a table fan).

![](images/4ace57983436c19afdbf186189ea14c2aff6c74066692661699785c42946e8f1.jpg)  
P48 Courtesy of Karl Ernst Osthaus Museum, Hagen

The cast-iran matar housing was painted green, with tambac bands; the blades were palished brass plate.

## Low-Speed Ceiling Fan ca. 1908

In this two-blade DC fan made for direct ceiling mounting, the molded cube once again acted as the connection between the mounting plate and the motor (see P51).

## Types

Two types were offered: height 325 mm, blade diameter 1,090 mm, mounting plate diameter 200 mm; height 375 mm, blade diameter 1,240 mm, mounting plate diameter 250 mm.

## Construction and Finish

Cast-iron casing painted green, with tombac bands; wooden blades.

P66 Ceiling Fan ca. 1908

Two-bladed, low-speed AC ceiling fon for direct mounting. The broad, deep groves cut into the ceiling rose join together in the pommel under the motor. Unlike P57-P60, the blodes were not ottoched to the pommel but fixed directly to the motor casing via ongle brockets. Voriotions on this design were available with hanging rods, light bulbs below the motor, ond fourbladed fans.

## Finish

Painted green. Tombac decorative bonds were ovoilable. Wooden blades.

## P71

## Wall Ventilator ca. 1908

Four-bladed, high-speed DC wall ventilator with two concentric steps on mounting plate and circle and square motif around bolt holes. This fan, which was also supplied in a six-bladed version, was intended for large rooms such as cafés, offices, and factory halls, and was installed in the outside wall or in the upper part of a window, so that the subtleties of design were barely visible. Three different motors were offered.

## Types

GB1 (25 watts): mounting plate diameter 330 mm, blade diameter 0 mm, 12 price 30 marks PL no. 66017.

GB2 (45 watts): mounting plate diameter 390 mm, blade diameter 300 mm, 1912 price 33.50 marks, PL no. 66027

GB3 (75 watts), mounting plate diameter 460 mm, blade diameter 360 mm, 1912 price 43 marks, PL no. 66037.

## Finish

Painted green with tombac rims. Green-painted sheet-steel blades. Also supplied in gray or with tombac plating.

## References

AEG photos 14430, 13166 (tombac-plated version); Int. AEG-Ztg. 12, no. 11 (May 1910), suppl. 6; AEG-Ztg. 12, no. 12 (June 1910), p. 11; Int. AEG-Ztg. 13, no. 12 (June 1911), suppl. 14; ibid. 14, no. 11 (May 19i2), suppl. 8 (with price list and drawing); ibid. 16, no. 10 (April 1914), suppl.; Elektrizität in Haushalt und Gewerbe, AEG brochure (1914), p. 62.

![](images/3d7e09544ff0a1899ff67c298db30fd8ccd05ea0ddc1a406a3d68fc303f76866.jpg)  
P71

![](images/58b71a8f3cd2b2e27b641631f2307790e5d300b3ab095c03ef4a958e392e6882.jpg)  
P72

## P72

## Wall Ventilator

ca. 1908

Four-bladed, high-speed AC wall ventilator with iris diaphragm mechanism. The mounting plate had three concentric steps with three protruding bolt lugs decorated with a square motif. The diaphragm served to alter the size of the vent and worked on the same principle as the aperture diaphragm on a camera. It was operated by cords running over pulleys. Three different motors were offered.

## Types

WB1 (35 watts): mounting plate diameter 330 mm, blade diameter 250 mm, 1912 price 30 marks, PL no. 66317

WB2 (70 watts): mounting plate diameter 390 mm, blade diameter 300 mm, 112 price 54 marks, P no. 66327

WB3 (85 watts): mounting plate diameter 460 mm, blade diameter 360 mm, 1912 price 80 marks, PL no. 66337.

## Finish

As P71.

## References

AEG photo 14443; Int. AEG-Ztg. 14, no. 11 (May 1912), suppl. 8 (with design drawing); ibid. 16, no. 10 (April 1914), suppl.; MdBEW 10, no. 6 (June 1914), p. 91; Elektrizität in Haushalt und Gewerbe, AEG brochure (1914); p. 62; Scheibe 1965, p. 203 (dated around 1912).

## P73

## Wall Ventilator

ca. 1903

Four-bladed, high-speed DC wall ventilator with self-acting louver shutter. As a result of their lightness, the mica louvers opened and closed according to the stream of air passing through the ventilator. louver shutters were available for all the AEG wall ventilators and were mounted on a ring which fitted over the body of the ventilator.

Types s P71.

Finish As P71.

## References

AEG photo 16390 (AEG-Telefunken Archive); Elektrizitat in Haushalt und Gewerbe, AEG brochure (1914), p. 63.

![](images/97b645a81a2072399d65dd6c2fcbf0265e8026d1d66e3afe268b62b4ff69cb44.jpg)  
P73

## P74

## Table Fan and Centrifugal Fan

ca. 1908

The table fan (left) was designed to such in and disperse smoke. The centrifugal fan (right) pumped a small volume of air at a relatively high pressure, so that it was particularly suitable for supplying air along pipes or tubes or for cleaning air containing steam or dust. Both types were DC.

## Finish

Painted green with tombac bands.

## References

AEG photo 13528; MdBEW 6, no.   
5 (May 1910), pp. 68, 70.

P76

## • P77 Humidifier ca. 1 1909

Electric water atamizer to humidify the air, mounted on fixed stand. The wire guard was dispensed with on this madel, as the moisture was prapelled through the jets at the center of the machine as a fine mist, withaut farming drops. The design stressed the technical aspects of the humidifier; the moldings on the stand and motor casing and the tambac bands were the only decorative features (see P79). This humidifier was offered in AC and DC madels, at a 1911 price af 80 marks.

## Finish

Painted green with tambac banks.

## References

AEG photos 16690, 16684; Int. AEG-Ztg. 14, no. 2 (August 191 1), suppl. (with price list); MdBEW 11, no. 7-8 (July-August 1915), p. 128 (annauncement).

![](images/cfad12a9f5d774bd25909e807f1787e9facb1dac42d83f6e611b1071f346d71b.jpg)

ca. 1909

As P79. The bowl and motor casing had a decorative tombac cladding.

## References

Hagen photo 14340; Breuer 1910, p. 266; JDW 1912, p. 66 (described as fan and illustrated upside down); Lotz 1932 (described as fan and illustrated upside down).

![](images/99250fcdf93361ec8e296ef18646d099b060b7cf41d26fb3a1ace608546522fe.jpg)  
P78 Caurtesy af Karl Ernst Osthaus Museum, Hagen.

## P79 Humidifier 1909

Sprayer to humidify the air, mounted on a fixed stand and with a wire guard. An electrically driven cone rotated inside the bowl. The resulting centrifugal force threw the water up onto the fins, where it was atomized by the draft from the rotating fan blades attached to the cone. A simple, grooved motor casing supported the wide bowl, which held the safety guard. This appliance was intended for dry, dusty rooms or for disinfection purposes.

Finish Painted green with tombac bands.

## References

AEG photo 14339; Hagen photo V 236/2; AEG-Ztg. 12, no. 2 (AUgust 1909), pp. 23; MdBEW 6, no. 12 (December 1910), p. 182; Breuer 1910, p. 266; Hoeber 1913, p. 108, illustration 118 (dated 1909).

## P81

## Grinding and Polishing Motor

ca. 1908-09

Sealed matar with dustpraaf bearings maunted an a fixed stand with taper spindles at bath ends af the drive shaft for the grinding and polishing attachments. The machine was principally used by watchmakers, jewelers, and galdsmiths. The DC madel had shunt winding, sa that the speed af the matar cauld be regulated.

## Finish

Painted green with tombac bands.

## References

## P83-P85 Kitchen Motor ca. 1911

This electric kitchen motor was mounted on a broad base plate. An adjustable driveshaft could be attached to various kitchen appliances via a spring-loaded clutch, so that the motor could be used to cut bread and salami, stir mayonnaise, or mince meat. The simple form of the casing amplified the mechanical qualities of the appliance and was reminiscent of the polishing machine shown in P81 and P82.

Finish As P81.

## References

AEG-Telefunken photo ZWA/Fo R 1045 (dated 1911); MdBEW 7, no. 4 (April 1911), pp. 5154; ibid. 10, no. 1 (January 1914), back cover; ibid. 11, no. 3 (March 1915), back cover.

## P86, P87 Dental Drill 1908

The electric wire was led over two pulleys along a pivoting arm attached to a wooden mounting plate. The square-circle-square motif and the stepped cube used on the fans and ventilators also appeared here as decorative elements (see P45, P47, P48), and the casing of the small electric motor was given a harmonious, spherical form.

P95

## Standard Control Panel (Frame, ca. 190708)

Cantrol panel far ane three-phase, low-voltoge generatar and ten circuits, with clack mounting ond clock (not by Behrens). These cantral panels were mode up of several verticol bands, each far ane circuit ar one group of circuits. The bands were made af three sectians and were 650 mm or 750 mm wide. Each band camprised an upper panel with meters; o middle ponel with switches, fuses, and regulatars far thot particulor circuit; ond a lower ponel to serve as a base, ar ta carry cantrals that rarely had ta be ottended to. This loyaut cauld be extended at will, ond the marble plates were cut fram ane black ta abtain the same pattern and caloring. In spite of this visual attentiveness, anly the design af the waoden frame con be definitively ascribed ta Behrens.

Materials and Construction Front side: highly palished marble panels. Back side: unpalished marble panels. Enamel varnish; cannections of tin-ploted copper (oll water-resistant materials).

## References

AEG photas 7204 (13 August 1910), 8609, 8795 (18 Morch 1912-a smaller, madified version built far the Technische Hachschule, Charlottenburg); Deutsche Schiffbau-Ausstellung, 1908, AEG exhibitian cotalog (see A1-A8); MdBEW 5, na. 3 (March 1909), p. 46, illustrotian; ibid. 7, no. 7 (July 191 1), p. 107 (withaut clack); Elektrizität in Haushalt und Gewerbe, AEG brochure (1914), p. 87; AEG Eisenbahn Abteiling, Elektrische Stellwerke für Winden und Signale, 1910 editian, p. 10; Int. AEG-Ztg. 14, na. 12 (July 1912), suppl. 3, p. 21; Enciclopedia universale dell'arte, val. 7 (1958), plote 290.

![](images/59ffe40deb8a1a210615b612a92f2575036789e67b6a805501ea557501c43d71.jpg)

## P97

Standard Control Panel (Frame, ca. 190708)

Cantral panel withaut clack, made af metal campanents which cauld be easily assembled with clamps, screws, etc. The twa upper panels in the marble version were replaced here by ane panel af sheet metal. Marble cauld nat be used in dusty surraundings such as mines, and slate ar sheet metal had ta be substituted. The influence af Behrens at least appears in the waaden malding between the metal plates.

Finish Iran plate.

References   
AEG phata 8609; MdBEW 12, na. 9 (September 1916), pp.   
136-137.

## P98 Instrument Column ca. 190809

Calumn supporting five measuring instruments. There were three basic types, with scape far individual variatians: Madel GSA was far a maximum af five instruments. It was alsa passible to extend the headpiece, especially far use with generators, with a switch built in at the tap af the calumn. This switch was alsa available an models GS and R. Madel GS, without crossbar, was intended far transfarmers and the like. Madel R was a simple design for a maximum of three instruments. Behrens's influence was limited ta the design af the calumn. A large cube carrying the AEG trademark formed the connectian between the calumn and the instrument mountings, and two smaller cubes linked the crossbar to the valtmeters and ammeters an each side. There is na evidence that Behrens wasinvalved in the design af the instruments.

## Construction

Gas pipe.

## References

AEG phata, unnumbered (AEG-Telefunken archivel; MdBEW 5, na. 3 (March 1909), p. 46 (calumn with switch, installed in AEG administration building, Friedrich-Karl-Ufer); AEG-Ztg. 13, na. 8 (February 1911), suppl. 5; Franz 1917, p. 149.

## P100, P101 Pendulum Clocks 1910

These electric moster clocks were especially occurate, ond were used to set other clocks by or to control o series of slove clocks. The moster clock hod o second pendulum, which completed 60 swings per minute ond wos linked to the clockwork by on endless chain. The long pendulum, visible through the three glass ponels on the front of the wooden cose, guoronteed occurocy. Behrens stripped the housing ond the clock foce of oll unnecessory ornoment, and removed the customory orrowhead pointers from the honds.

## Types

N2: low-voltage woll clock, 1912 price 250 marks, Pl no. 4011 SN2: low-voltage stonding clock, 1912 price 485 morks, PL no. 4012

N1: high-voltoge woll clock, 1912 price 212 morks, PL no. 4036 SN2: high-voltage stonding clock, 1912 price 415 morks, PL no. 4035.

P102 Slave Clock ca. 1910

In the law-valtage versian, the slave clack was driven directly by the electrical impulses received fram the master clack, which were transmitted ta the hands via a cail assembly and a gathering click. The high-valtage versian had an independent clackwark mechanism with a self-acting electric winder, and was autamatically regulated every haur by the master clack. Bath madels were alsa supplied with auter casings (see P103-P105).

## .PIll Time Clock for Workplaces ca. 1910

This clock could work independently or be controlled by a master clock. The clockwork and the stamping mechanism were linked by a drive shaft.

Types and 1924 prices Two different types were offered, with the same dimensions: height 916 mm, width 380 mm, depth of clock unit 175 mm, depth of base 260 mm.

K1, with independent mechanism and one-color ribbon: 680 marks. K1, with master-clock control and one-color ribbon: 700 marks. K2, with independent mechanism and two-color ribbon: 750 marks. K2, with master-clock control and two-color ribbon: 770 marks.

## .P112 Independent and Master Clocks ca. 1910

Two independent clocks la and d; see P108) and two pendulum master clocks. The independent clocks could be supplied to order as table or mantelpiece models, like d. Behrens exhibited this model in the reception room he designed for the Keller and Reiner Gallery in Berlin. While P112b was the same as P100, the octagonal housing of the face gave an added elegance to P112c.

See P100 and P108.

## P113

## Convection Heater 1909

Portable AC heater with two settings, in ornamental cast-iron housing. In contrast to the filament heaters, which were intended for use over brief periods in small rooms, the convector heaters were intended for constant use.

Convection Heaters

The AEG price lists of 191 1 and 1912 contained convection heoters that connot definitely be oscribed to Behrens. His involvement can be conjectured, however, on the grounds of the close similority with P113-P115.

## P134-P137 Round Electric Kettles 1909

These electric kettles had a raunded farm, a raunded handle clad with waven cane attached ta circular lugs, and a shart, braad spaut set law an the bady. The increasing girth af the knab an the lid cantrasts directly against the taper af the kettle. This madel was alsa supplied withaut an electric heating element (see G53).

## Graphics

Gabriele Heidecker

## G1-G3

Cover Design and Title of Mitteilungen der Berliner Elektricitats-Werke 1906-07

This magazine appeared in Behrens's caver design with valume 2 (1906) and valume 3, na. 1 (January 1907). In the volumes covering 1907-1909, ane design was adhered ta thraughaut the year and only the color was changed far each issue.

Common ta all the designs is the basic geametric campasition af the frant caver and of the calendar on the back cover. The decarative cantents af the framewark varied from year to year: volume 3 (1907) has black circles, stylized scrolls and a square signet (G1), valume 4 (1908) has a pattern af superimposed circles and squares arranged concentrically araund a square signet (G2), and valume 5 (1909) has four rings with a zigzag matif set cancentrically around a circular signet (G3). The last issue ta appear in the Behrens farmat was valume 3, na. 11 (Navember 19091.

## Technique

Letterpress printing. Cover: Buchbinderei Wübben & Ca., Berlin, Wilhelmstrasse 9.

## Colors

Caver: gray-blue ground, black script and barder, beige frame. Title page: white graund with black script and barder; various colars.

## References

Original: AEG-Telefunken, Berlin; title page: Meyer-Schönbrunn 1913, illustrations an pp. 2224; van Maas 1976, illustratians 13, 14; Catalog Darmstadt, 1976, val. 2, no. 363; Buddensieg and Ragge 1, p., illustratian . Cover: MdBEW 4, na. 12 (December 1908), illustratian an p. 189; ibid. 5, no. 12 (December 1909), p. 190.

Cover of MdBEW, 1907.

## G4, G5

## Cover of Mitteilungen der Berliner Elektricitäts-Werke, Precursors and Successors

Even before the Behrens layout, MdBEW appeared each month in a different cover design and color, and with a calendar on the back cover [G4—designed by Emil von Doepler; signature E.D.; see Goldstein, Monogramm-lexicon (1964), p. 167]. Photographs of AEG products on the back cover and of AEG buildings on the title page first appeared with volume 7 (191 1). Although this format was pursued in the following years, only volume 7 had a cover design with script, decoration, and signet in the Behrens manner.

Technique As G1-G3.

Colors Various.

Reference

G7

## Title Page of AEG-Zeitung 1907

Hondwritten Behrens Roman script. The first issue in the new format wos volume 10, no. 1 (July 1907). This format was retoined until March 1909. Some alterotions then appeared as a result of the change of the title to Interne AEG-Zeitung, but the script remained unchanged. A special edition of the AEG-Zeitung printed in Behrens-Antiquo wos "AEG-ZEITUNG/ BER-LIN/ XI. JAHRG. N°. 10-APRIL 1909." The same script was olso used for the English longuage version, entitled AEG-JOURNAL.

Letterpress printing.

Dimensions 26.8 X 20.3 cm.

Colors Light gay card; black p.

Reference -elen.

G8

## G9, G10 AEG Exhibition Guide to Deutsche Schiffbau-Ausstellung 1908

This catalog to the AEG's special exhibition at the 1908 Schiffbau-Ausstellung had 15 pages wih 7 illustrations. The covers are shown above as illustration Al. Title page (G): Text as on front cover, each block of text in t own frame. Lower frame decorated with a diamond motif which emphasizes the central axis of the page ["Behrens Roman" decoration, as used on the back cover of AEG-Ztg. 11, nos. 10 ff. (April 1909-October 09. Text: Yellow frame around every page. Ornament on p. 3 made up of six identical elements. Decorated initial D on . 4 at start of description of exhibition (G10). The same D also appeared with a different border in the Rathenau address of 1907 (G24), the 25thanniversary Festschrift, 1908, and the invitation card of the AEG salesroom in Königgratzer Strasse, 1910 (P1).

## Technique

Letterpress printing, Georg Büxenstein und Comp., Berlin.

## Typefaces

Cover: Roman, handwritten by erens. Text: rst known example of the Behrens Roman typeface, with accompanying ornament, introduced October 1908.

## Dimensions

17.7 x 12.4 cm. Frame on front cover 16.2 x 11 cm, on title page 14.2 x 4.9 cm, on text pages 14 x 9.4 cm.

## Colors

Cover: chamois-colored ground with black lettering, graphics, a inner frames; outer frame yellow. Text pages: black lettering and yellow frames.

## References

Peter Behrens, letter of 23 September 1909, KEO Archive, Hagen IKu 418/120); Stoedtner photo 69 161; Zeitschrift für Bücherfreunde 2 (1910), illustrations 2, 12; Buddensieg and Rogge 1976, p. 113, nos. 14, 15; Buddensieg and Rogge 1977, p. 4.

## DEUTSCHE SCHIFFBAU-AUSSTELLUNG 1908

## ALLGEMEINE ELEKTRICITAETS GESELLSCHAFT

![](images/c41925e4d1866fbc0a04dfa67a994bd6667c408e22a3273642d75a516292fb12.jpg)

er Blick des Besuchers fallt zunächst auf den in natürlicher Gröe und bordmäig gehaltenen vorderen Maschinenraum eines Torpedobootes. Es ist mitDampfturbinen und, dem Kolbenmaschinen - Betrieb analog, nur mit zwei Wellen und Propellern

ausgerüstet. Wenn auch die entsprechend groe Kolbendampfmaschine zum Vergleich feht, so erkennt man doch an der einfachen Bauart die Vorzuge des neuen Motors für Fahrzeuge, auf denen jeder Kubikmeter Raum ausgenutzt, der Maschinendienst im Interesse der aufs äuBerste angestrengten Mannschaft möglichst vereinfacht werden mu.

Der Beschauer sieht die Steuerbord-Turbine links, den zugehörigen Kondensator rechts, während der dahinterliegende Raum die Backbord-Turbine mit ihrem Kondensator in umgekehrter Anordnung enthält; die Schraubenwelle der ersten Maschine läuft also unter dem Kondensator der zweiten hindurch. Jede Turbine leistet bei 6oo Umdrehungen i. d. Minute 600o bis 7ooo PS und treibt ihre Welle vollkommen unabhängig von der anderen Maschine. Das ist ein Vorteil des hier angewandten Turbinensystems, der sich namentlich beim Manövrieren dadurch geltend macht, da für den Uebergang vom Vorwärts- zum Rückwärtslauf keine komplizierten Zwischenrohrleitungen, die den

## •G12, G13 AEG Twenty-Fifth Anniversary Festschrift 1908

Cover (G12): like the squore ploquette with the eogle (by H. Hohn?), the title "AEG/ 1883-1908" is firmly ploced within o rigid fromework, which stonds on the cover like o rectongulor block. (The some ploquette oppeored in on embossed version on the brown posteboord cover of o volume of onniversory songs: "12 Dezember 1908".) Title (G13): hondwritten Romon. A norrow, two-bonded frome set well in from the edge of the poge surrounds the text ond the illustrotions throughout the Festschrift, ond the poper is creomcolored. Poge 7 (not shown): Aport from the border, the Behrens Romon initiol D is the some os those shown in G10 ond P1.

## Dating

As the onniversory of the compony's founding fell on 14 April 1908, the Festschrift must hove been completed by this dote.

Brown cloth cover with embossed script ond fine lines. Ploquette held by four closps stomped out of zinc sheet.

## G14

## .G15-G17 Three Sheets with Various Scripts Peter Behrens

Script designs for titles, for use in brochures, pamphlets, price lists, etc. As the letters did not exist in print form and had to be adjusted to fit the differing formats of the publications, they were written out by hand each time. Discrepancies in the spacing and form of the lettering were a natural consequence. However, intentional alterations in the serifs and a tendency in G17 toward gentler, more rounded contours can also be observed. (ln G15 and G16 the horizontal and vertical strokes form a perfect right angle; in G17 the angle has been rounded off by subsequent correction.) The numbers in G16 were used by Behrens on his clock faces; see P106.

## Technique

Pen, ink, and zinc white on card strips.

## Dimensions

Average length of line 23 cm; letters of varying heights; numbers in G16 1.9 cm.

## Reference

Private collection, Berlin.

G18   
Design for Sans-Serif   
Script   
Peter Behrens

This sans-serif script is lighter and more closely spaced than the standard script in the AEG Normalien (1916), no. 120, pp. 89/90. The lower-case S is narrower, and the lower-case R has a rolled-up hook. Compare the upper-case letters with the inscription on the window of the Königgrätzer Strasse showroom (P7).

Technique   
Pen and black ink, with corrections with zinc white, on lined parchment paper.

Dimensions 15 x 46 cm; height of letters 2 cm.

References   
Original: private collection, Berlin; AEG-Normalien (1916), no. 120, pp. 89/90.

## Allgemeine Elektrizitts Gefellchaft PROF. PETER BEHRENS NEUBABELSBERG Hamburg Amerika Linie

G19   
Design for Alphabet   
Peter Behrens

Upper-case alphabet in Roman script, with the words "diese machen Worte zeichnen" underneath in Behrens's handwriting. The initials for the AEG trademark were taken from this alphabet (see G80), and it was used in numerous versions in brochures, posters, advertisements, and inscriptions. (A page of pencil sketches in the Behrens papers show early studies, and the note "poliphil" points to the model for this script: the "Hypnerotomachia Poliphili" of Aldus Manutius, Venice, 1499.

Technique Pencil on parchment.

Dimensions   
19 x 26.5 cm; height of letters 2 cm.

Reference nalprivat oletinBn.

# G23-G25 Certificate to Mark Award of Grashof Memorial Medal to Emil Rathenau Peter Behrens

Two parchment sheets bound in punched, colored leather, with handworked inscription, initials, and ornamentation and gald leaf decoration.

## References

Original, according to information received from AEG-Telefunken Archive, disappeared in Braunschweig. Marburg photos 616435 (front cover); 616436 (inside pages); MdBEW 3, na. 1 (January 1908), illustration on p. 9, pp. 10 ff.; Kunst und Künstler 6, na. 10 (June 1908); Gustav E. Pazaurek, "Maderne Ehrenurkunden," Dekorative Kunst 14, no. 1 (October 1910), pp. 25 ff., illustration 32; Meyer-Schönbrunn 1913, illustration on p. 35; Deutsche Werkbundausstellung, Basel, 1917.

![](images/8fa45c6343ebd84a5287d209dc8d99a08e26562d164c7f19aa1d7c6b0e29d48d.jpg)

Leather cover of certificate. The initials are comparable to the AEG signet; see G78. The ornamentation in the spandrel-shaped corners and the rectangular pattern filling the rest of the frame belong to the decorative elements that went with the Behrens Roman script, introduced in 1908. Marburg photo.

![](images/0f5c16e1e8e25865702e72a46fbc023ab07a61a7c13b519e196d0640897f80b6.jpg)  
G24  
First inside page of certificate. With slight variatians, the initials D and H and the decarative elements are all taken from the Behrens Raman repertoire, although the lettering is Didane with ligatures. Marburg phata.

![](images/5dada1a0e811858a3cba9cc7df5df9f9a6450ef0512e09a0a04475a41ab20aa2.jpg)  
G25 Second page. Marburg phata.

## Congratulatory Address from BEW on Twenty-Fifth Anniversary of AEG 1908

Two parchment sheets bound in dark blue leather, with handworked inscription, initials, and ornamentation and gold leaf decoration. The initials E, A, and M, the decorative elements, and the pattern used to fill the last line of the address all correspond substantially to the Behrens Roman typeface and decorations, introduced in October 1908.

## Colors

Apart from the introductory sentence and the initials, the script is black, framed with violet and with green ornamentation. The initials are either all gold, gold and blue, or gold and red.

## References

MdBEW 4, no. 5 (5 May 1908), p. 73 (announcement); ibid. 4, no. 6 (June 1908), illustration on p. 82; Jaumann 1909, illustrations on pp. 352 (script) and 353 (front and back covers); Gustav E. Pazaurek, "Moderne Ehrenurkunden," Dekorative Kunst 14, no. 1 (October 10), pp.  ff. illustration n . 32; Meyer-Schönbrunn 1913, illustration on p. 36; Hoeber 1913, illustration 79, p. 75; Internationale Ausstellung fur Buchgewerbe und Graphik, Leipzig, 1914, Katalog der Abt. Neuzeitliche Buchkunst U. angew. Graphik, illustration on p. 25.

## G57

## Prospectus: "Elektrische Uhren'

## Peter Behrens

This design may have preceded G56, as the text here is handwritten whereas that in G56 is typeset.

## Technique

Letterpress printing. Lettering in negative frames and clock in positive relief printing.

## Dimensions

Card 25.7 x 24.6 cm; frame 19.2 x 18.8 cm.

## Colors

Brown relief card; broad frame, rectangle containing oval frame and outline of clock black; dots and inner frames gold; medallion light violet; clock gray-green; clock face white with numbers and AEG signet designed by Behrens.

## Reference

Original: Kunstbibliothek, Berlin.

![](images/36954dd1a1d77f31c3252453fef1869ab22b94ebb10a8924859d79cac288de3c.jpg)

Supplement: "Elektrische Uhren'

Louis Oppenheim, 1913, using forms designed by Behrens

Representation of a clock at night. Illuminated numbers and clock hands taken from a Behrens-designed clock. The same motif also appeared as a publicity seal (see G74) and as the title illustration of the brochure "Abbildungen ausgefuhrter Uhren-Anlagen" Int. AEG-Ztg. 16, no. 8 (February 1914), suppl.. The latter brochure bore the monogram "LO."

Technique Letterpress printing.

Dimensions 26.5 X 20.8 cm.

Colors

"AEG" white, with dark blue edging; lettering white with black hatching, border and clock hands blue; numbers green; ground blueblack.

## Reference

Original: Int. AEG-Ztg. 14, no. 11 (May 1912), suppl. 9; MdVDR, no. 45 (0ctober 1913), p. 341 (small illustration with monogram "LO").

![](images/65b7fc09f0c0987391e3331897d86f2af97d93a1f4f5621c8671d68c320e4a4c.jpg)

## Announcements for AEG Products

Peter Behrens (?), 190910

This series of announcements appeared in various borders on the back cover of the magazine Mitteilungen der Berliner Elektricitäts-Werke. The series began with MdBEW 5, no. 12 (December 1909) and ran regularly on the next twelve issues [6, nos. 112 (1910)].

## G74

## Publicity Seals

These designs can be ascribed ta Behrens with certainty anly where they can be related ta his paster, pamphlet, ar brachure illustratians: an "Metalldraht-Lampe" see G31 and G32; an "Ventilataren" see G55; an "Elektrische Uhren" see G58; an "Nitralampe" see G46 and G47.

## Dimensions

Rectangular seals 6 x 4 cm; hexagonal seal 8 cm high and 6.5 cm wide, with 3.8-cm sides.

## References

Originals: Sammlung Kaurist, Bann; Heissluftdusche" in same farmat in praspectus in Int. AEG-Ztg. 16, na. 7 (January 1914), suppl. The madel far the hexaganal seal, a demanstratian panel with sets af fuses, was phatagraphed in the exhibitian raam af the electricalappliance factary (P13) and in the AEG display at the 191 1 Munich exhibitian [see MdBEW 7, na. 7 (July 1911), pp. 99ff., illustration 21.

## AEG Trademark Franz Schwechten, 1896

Copyright applied for 11 June 1898, registered as no. 34660 in class 42 on 6 December 1898. Inlaid into "management gate" at 107a Brunnenstrasse, built to Schwechten's design in 1896. Also used concurrently with new, Behrens-designed versions, for example in the AEG pavilion at the Allgemeine Ausstellung von Erfindungen der Klein-Industrie in 1907. See MdBEW 3, no. 9 (September 1907), illustrations on p. 132 (Schwechten trademark), pp. 130, 131, 135 (Behrens trademark). See also A9.

## G86

## Winning Entries in AEG Poster Contest

Eight postcards showing the prizewinning designs. The open competition for a poster advertising the AEG nitra lamp was announced in Kunstgewerbeblatt 27, no. 7 (191516), p. 140. The members of the jury were named as Peter Behrens, Curt Herrmann, Emil Orlik, E. R. Weiss, Paul Mamroth, Walther Rathenau, and Ernst Salomon. The results and illustrations of the winning entries were published in MdBEW 12, no. 6 (June 1916), pp. 87-90.

Technique Letterpress printing.

Dimensions 10 × 7.5 cm.

## Reference

Originals: private collection, Berlin.

# A Leading Figures of the AEG, 1883-1928

Tilmann Buddensieg

In the following section are assembled portraits and short biographical details of the men who determined the technical, commercial, and financial policies of the AEG in the period 1883-1928. Owing to the lack of information and of preliminary studies, this list cannot claim to be complete or exhaustive. Also included are portraits of Peter Behrens between 1906 and 1928.

## Emil Rathenau

Berlin, 11 December 1838Berlin, 20 June 1915

See the essay by Henning Rogge earlier in this volume.

![](images/25878155210f69c60df53e67c48af8b7653cdcbff82e85592cbf707185f3b428.jpg)

Appendix A ·486

## Felix Deutsch Breslau, 16 May 1858Berlin, 19 May 1928

In the winter of 1882-83, Deutsch met Emil Rathenau; on 19 April 1883 the Deutsche Edison-Gesellschaft für angewandte Elektricität was founded. The company began business at 94 Leipziger Strasse with one bookkeeper and one typewriter. In 1915, Deutsch succeeded Emil Rathenau as chairman of the managing board of the AEG. See Wenzel, Deutsche Wirtschaftsführer (Berln, 1929); Felix Pinner, Emil Rathenau (1918), p. 397; Felix Pinner (Frank Fassland), "Felix Deutsch," Deutsche Wirtschaftsführer (Berlin, 1924), pp. 197 ff. The last-named article gives a detailed account of Deutsch's responsibilities as the initiator of the customer-service department, which employed Peter Behrens to design products, shops, pamphlets, and sales catalogs:

It is well known that Rathenau was a rare mixture of engineer, businessman and financier. His roots as a businessman were not to be found in the commercial sphere, but in industry, which meant that he was less interested in selling the products as such and in sales techniques and more interested in manufacturing a product in such a way that it could be sold easily. The product, he felt, should be able to stimulate market demand, to satisfy existing needs or promote the needs of the future. But the technically successful manufacture of a satisfactory or even superior product was not enough on its own to stimulate a new area of demand, and the sales of an innovative product could not be left to conventional marketing techniques. It was essential to display the new goods and describe them in such a way that their merits were made clear to the individual customer. This propaganda for new ideas and products belonged—at least at the beginning—to the branch of industry in which Emil Rathenau was active. . . . The marketing of standard products and mass articles, of the machine-made goods that made up the largest part of a firm's turnover, was. another matter. . . . But the fact that the AEG also pursued new directions in this area as well can be ascribed to the influence of Felix Deutsch, who was with the firm from its inception. Deutsch's commercial talent effectively complemented the abilities of Rathenau, which lay more in the direction of production engineering. When foced with o questian he could nat salve in his awn individual way, Rathenau had a certain admiration and predilectian far American salutians. The Americon marketing system wos based an private sales representotives, cammissian agents, and electricians, wha were responsible far selling the standard, mass-praduced gaads ta the custamers. In this respect, Deutsch was mare lagical and forsighted than Rathenou himself. . . . The three hundred technical and sales bureaus Deutsch apened in central lacatians bath at hame and abraod develaped inta the moin supports of the soles arganizotion and made it passible ta bring oll the new praducts onta the morket by the mast direct raute. The bureaus were staffed by salesmen and engineers, wha nat anly instolled the equiment spplid by he AEG but  servid t regularly and gested passible additians and impravements ta the plant. In shart, the bureous provided the customers with the some service thot would atherwise have been given by sa-called "cansulting engineers."

See alsa Hermann Bücher, in Deutsches Biographisches Jahrbuch 10 (1928); Spannung—Die AEG-Umschau 1 (1928), speciol edition, with Deutsch's reminiscences.

## Paul Mamroth

## Breslau, 20 September 1859-20 November 1938

Mamrath was intimately linked with the firm fram 1887 an. He wos Directar and a member af the monoging baard until 1928, then a member af the supervisory boord. Pinner [Emil Rathenau (1918), p. 397] gives an - cant af Mamrath's respansibilities:whalesaling, retailing, accauntng cashier's department, financial planning, supervisian af subsidiaries—a Minister of the Interiar, so to speak." Infarmation on Mamrath was kindly given by Hons Fürstenberg.

![](images/77b24e3a22e6ac03828784421fd62e60ebfc9cbb9c90f2daf7088f258c3b341b.jpg)

![](images/0e6136daf4a426f9df400cbb7dea4d7cf4c7330aafbc18761bf636bf831b6145.jpg)  
Paul Mamroth (detail from group photograph), ca. 191012.  
Paul Mamroth. From Reichshandbuch der Deutschen Gesellschaft, vol. 2 (Berlin, 1931), p. 1184.

The engineer Paul Jordan, who joined at the founding of the Deutsche Edison Gesellschaft in 1883, was another of Emil Rathenau's longest-serving associates. Jordan had worked in the Patent Office and was initially head of the AEG's patent department. He was later responsible for all the AEG factories in Germany lexcept for the cable works), and for the factories that were linked to foreign subsidiaries. See Pinner, Emil Rathenau (1918), p. 398. Jordan was a member of the AEG's managing board until his retirement in 1920. In 1911 he was given the honorary title of Baurat (government surveyor of works). According to Edmund Schüler, "Behrens and Jordan were similar in their boundless, domineering energy. Their powerful figures attracted attention wherever they went." (Schüler was then a legation councillor in the Foreign Ministry, and was Behrens's "client" for the embassy in St. Petersburg. His memoirs are in the Foreign Ministry Archives, Bonn.) Among other positions, Jordan was on the supervisory board of the Deutsche Werft AG, where he met and became friendly with Paul Reusch of the Gutehoffnungshütte, Oberhausen [see Erich Maschke, E entsteht ein Konzern. Paul Reusch und di GHH (Tübingen, 199, pp. 455, 209 ff. Jordan also recommended Behrens to Reusch to design the new buildings of the GHH in Oberhausen, which were begun in 1921. [This information was kindly supplied by Herr Bodo Herzog, the archivist of the GHH. See also Spannung—Die AEG-Umschau 2 (July 1929), p. 300] Petra Behrens has kindly confirmed that Jordan appears on the left of illustration 11, in front of Paul Mamroth.

## Carl Fürstenberg Danzig, 28 August 1850-Berlin, 9 February 1933

As Managing Director of the Berliner Handelsgesellschaft, Fürstenberg played an important role in the initial financing of the AEG. In 1887 he was made a member of the supervisory board; in 1896 he became deputy chairman, and later he was chairman. He was a friend of Emil and Walther Rathenau and Felix Deutsch, all of whom were on the governing board of the Berliner Handelsgesellschaft. See Wenzel, Deutsche Wirtschaftsführer (1929); Carl Fürstenberg, Die Lebensgeschichte eines deutschen Bankiers 1930; second edition, ed. Hans Fürstenberg, 1968. See also Neue Deutsche Biographie, 5 (1961), s.v. Hans Fürstenberg.

Photo of Corl Fürstenberg. From Reichshandbuch, vol. 1, p. 511.

![](images/5eac63a73497fbe000f02f22458b79e511f7de3a826cc14b17b9e2e669bae993.jpg)  
Drowing of Corl Fürstenberg done by Fritz Grossmann in 1930 for Fürstenberg's eightieth birthdoy. From the collection of Hons-Jürgen Imielo, Mainz, who kindly provided the photogroph ond informotion.

![](images/5d9578bbce9bdd273344fa1b38c2af42dae18b546ac6729a60bb64de5ccd8a77.jpg)

## Georg Klingenberg Hamburg, 28 November 1870-Berlin, 7 December 1925

Klingenberg, a lecturer at the Technische Hachschule in Charlattenburg, was a member af managing baard af the AEG fram 1902 ta 1925. Mare than seventy power statians were built ta his plans. Amang ather things, Klingenberg wrate Der Bau grosser Elektrizitätswerke (three valumes, 191314, 1920; secand editian 1924; reprint 1926) and "Das neuzeitliche Elektrizitatswerk," Zeitschrift des Vereins Deutscher Ingenieure 65 (1925). See alsa Gedenkschrift Georg Klingenberg (Berlin, 1925).

Michael von Dolivo-Dobrowolsky St. Petersburg, 2 January 1862-Darmstadt, 15 November 1919

See "Madern Mass Praductian . . . " (translated in this valume).

Georg Klingenberg. From Gedankschrift Klingenberg (1925).

![](images/f0595efc23129fd255df09cc9378b009136171d231459ce77a03eed569c5bfa1.jpg)  
Michael von Dolivo-Dobrowolsky. From Das goldene Buch des Deutschen Volkes an der Jahrhundertwende (Leipzig, no date; 1901?), p. 37.

## Oskar Lasche Leipzig, 22 June 1868Berlin, 30 June 1923

Lasche studied mechanical engineering at the Technische Hachschule in Charlattenburg. In 1889 he became assistant ta Riedler, and fram there he went ta the AEG as head engineer in the AEG drafting affice. In 1902 he became directar af the machine factary in Brunnenstrasse. Fram 1903 until his death he was directar af the turbine factary in Huttenstrasse and in 1912 he alsa became a deputy member af the managing baard. Lasche wrate several articles an the turbine-factary pawerhause and the new turbine hall, an which he callabarated at the planning stage with Bernhard and Behrens. He later described the turbine hall as a "camplete success." See "Das Kraftwerk der AEG Turbinenfabrik in Berlin," Zeitschrift des Vereins Deutscher Ingenieure 53 (21 April 1909), pp. 648 ff.: The relatively small pawerhause is natable far its simplicity and clarity. . . .". See alsa ibid., pp. 699 ff.; "Die neue Turbinenhalle der AEG," AEG-Zeitung 12 (January 1910), pp. 1 ff.; "Ein gang durch die AEG-Turbinenfabrik," AEG-Zeitung 14 (July-September 1911), pp. 1 ff.; Walter Kieser, Deutsches Biographisches Jahrbuch (1923), pp. 240 ff.; AEG Berlin, 25 Jahre AEG Dampfturbinen (Berlin, 1928); 50 Jahre AEG (Berlin, 1956), index.

![](images/83a616d483b9d9b08f0cec140fbd41a6e61be58e4261c45ce9c4360b7615daf8.jpg)

![](images/dc0433f7cff01361201274d66bc0b8e85f409463291580f904bea11b58e4f21c.jpg)  
Michael von Dolivo-Dobrowolsky. Deutsches Museum, Munich.

Michael von Dolivo-Dobrowolsky. From Elektrotechnische Zeitschrift 41 (1920), p. 12.

![](images/04be8c561142810dac895767d6fe98d577379fefc393995e303ee187022b3f16.jpg)

Oskar Lasche. From 25 Jahre AEG Dampfturbinen (Berlin, 1928).

![](images/d0c14599a3b0a789be37bce2bcaef4599789143fc94b287a95de2532041f32ad.jpg)

## Georg Stern Königsberg, 11 June 1867-?

Stern was the head of the testing and meter-construction department of the Union Elektricitats-Gesellschaft. When this firm was taken over by the AEG, he became the head of the testing department and the laboratories in the machine factory. In 1908 he became manager of the high-voltage factory, in 1921 director of the transformer factory in Oberschöneweide, and in 1926 a deputy member of the managing board (later he was a full member). See Georg Wenzel, Deutsche Wirtschaftsführer (1929); J. Biermanns and O. Mayr leds.), Hochspannungsforschung und Hochspannungspraxis, Georg Stern zum 31. März 1931 (Berlin, 1931).

B The Behrens Studio in Neu-Babelsberg

This photogroph must hove been token before 1910, os the figure in the foreground ot the extreme left is Wolter Gropius, who worked in Behrens's studio from June 1908 until March 1910 (see "Industriol Buildings," translated obove). The stonding figure in the bock connot, therefore, hove been le Corbusier as is often supposed, for he first came to Behrens in the summer of 1910. Besides Gropius, one con recognize Adolf Meyer, Ludwig Mies van der Rohe, ond perhops Jeon Krämer and Peter Grossmon. The two lost named were the outstonding tolents in the studio [see Max Osborn, Jean Krämer (Berlin, 1927), p. VIl; Werner Klinski, Peter Grossman (Berlin, 1973), p. 1]. Grossmon worked in Behrens's studio from 9 November 1908 until 30 October 1914. In April 1913, Schulenberg, the District President of Potsdom, reported in o letter to the Ministry of Public Works thot Behrens ron "on importont studio . .. which employs around thirty people, some at consideroble solories" (files on the St. Petersburg embossy, Foreign Ministry Archives, Bonn).

The Behrens studio, Neu-Babelsberg, before March 1910.
